[Column 3]
GENERAL INFORMATION.
	For ladies not wishing to stop at the
Maxwell House, comfortable quarters can
be secured at satisfactory rates in private
boarding-houses. Information upon this
subject is volunteered by the Chairman of
the Convocation Committee, Mrs. Margar-
et J. Weakley, whose address is 117 North
Summer Street, Nashville, Tenn.
	The rates made by the railroads to and
from Nashville during the Exposition are
much lower than the rates obtained by con-  
ventions. This advantage is, of course,
available to all persons attending the
Executive Session of the National Council.
	The importance of this Executive Ses-
sion can hardly be over estimated, and it
becomes the duty of each National Organ-
ization and Local Council connected with
the National Council to take its share in
making the session worthy of the Council
and of the distinguished auspices under
which this session will be held. This
Executive Session affords the Council the
opportunity to present what is popularly
known as the “Council Idea,’’ in a section
of the country not yet reached by it; and
before audiences that will be representative
of all that is best, particularly in the South-
ern States. It also gives every member of
the Council an opportunity to present its
particular work to an audience which will
carry a knowledge of it, and the inspira-
tion to be derived from it, to all parts of
the great and growing South. 
	This preliminary programme is sent out
in the hope that the important subjects pro-
posed for discussion may be considered by
Local Councils and, as far as possible, by
members of the official board of National
organizations before the session is convened.
	N. B.—Members of the Official Boards
are asked to consider these matters, because
it will not be possible to bring them before
their respective organizations.  
	For all information concerning the local
arrangements at Nashville, inquiries may
be addressed to Mrs. Margaret J. Weakley,
117 North Summer Street, Nashville, Tenn.
	Members of the Cabinet, Patrons of the
National Council and Local Councils are
requested to address any inquiries or sug-
gestions respecting the programme to Mrs. 
May Wright Sewall, whose address is
given below.
	All other persons interested in the Nash-
ville meeting are requested to address
inquiries and suggestions to the Chairman
of the Committee, Rev. Anna H. Shaw,
Vice-President at Large of the National
Council of Women, and Chairman of the
Programme Committee of the Nashville
Meeting, Wianno, Cape Cod, Mass.
	Rachel Foster Avery, Member of the
Cabinet. Hannah J. Solomon, President
of the National Council of Jewish Women.
Annie White Johnson Chairman of Com-
mittee on Dress. May Wright Sewall,
Member of Cabinet, 633 (new) North Penn-
sylvania Street, Indianapolis, Indiana.


THE three leading newspapers of Nash-
ville employ women reporters. Miss Walk-
er represents the Sun, Mrs. Duncan the
American and Miss Porterfield the Banner.
All are able and do credit to their calling.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


A ROSE TO THE LIVING.

A ROSE to the living is more 
	Than sumptuous wreaths to the dead:
In filling love’s infinite store,
A rose to the living is more—
If graciously given before
	The hungering spirit is fled— 
A rose to the living is more 
	Than sumptuous wreaths to the dead.
				SELECTED.


A SONG OF AUTUMN.

Ho for the bending sheaves,
Ho for the crimson leaves 
	Flaming in splendor!
Season of ripened gold,
Plenty in crib and fold,
Skies with depths untold,
	Liquid and tender.
Far. like the smile of God,
See how the golden-rod
	Ripples and tosses!
Yonder a crimson vine
Trails from a bearded pine,
Thin as a thread of wine 
	Staining the mosses.
Bright ’neath the morning blue
Sparkles the frosted dew,
	Gem-like and starry.
Hark how the partridge cock
Pipes to the scattered flock, 
Mindful how swift the hawk
	Darts on his quarry!
Autumn is here again—
Banners on hill and plain 
	Blazing and flying.
Hail to the amber morn, 
Hail to the heaped up corn,
Hail to the hunter's horn,
	Swelling and dying!
				JAMES BUCKHAM.


UNDER A HEDGE.

A FLORIST a sweet little blossom espied,
Which bloomed like its Ancestors by the road side,
Its fragrance was simple, its colors were few,
But the blossom looked fair in the spot
Where it grew. Under a Hedge. 

The Florist beheld it and cried “I’ll enchant
The Botanical world with this sweet little plant.
Its leaves shall be sheltered and carefully nursed,
It shall charm all the world tho’ I met with it first
			Under a Hedge.”

He carried it home to his hot-house with care 
And said “Tho’ the rarest exotics are there
My little pet plant when I’ve nourished its stem
In tints and in fragrance shall imitate them
And none shall suspect from the road side it
	came.
Rodum Sidus I’ll call it, a beautiful name,
And when Botanists look through their glasses to
	view
Its beauties they’ll never suspect that it grew
			Under a Hedge.”

The little pet plant when it shook off the dust
Of its own native ditch soon began to grow pert
And tossed its small head, for perceiving that none
But exotics were round it, it thought itself one
And forgot that it grew, Under a Hedge.

As a wild flower all would have owned it was
	fair,
And praised it though gaudier blossoms were
	there,
But when it assumes the hot-house airs we see
	through
The forced tints of its leaves and suspect that it
	grew
			Under a Hedge


In the by-ways of life oh how many there are, 
Who being born under a fortunate star, 
Assisted by beauty or talent grow rich, 
To bloom in a hot-house instead of a ditch.
And whilst they disdain not their own simple
	stem,
The honor they grasp may gain honor from them
But when like the pet plant such people grow
	pert, 
We soon trace them to their original birth
				Under a Hedge
						DILBHUS.


[Column 1]
MADAME MOUNTFORD.

WHAT THE CALIFORNIA PAPERS SAY.
	THAT Madame Mountford is a remark-
ably brilliant woman, and that she is a
great orator and actress, goes without say-
ing to all who were fortunate enough to
have heard her series of lectures at the
Yosemite theater last week, and that she is
withal a true, noble and Christian woman.
Her heart is in the work she has created
and although she has wonderful talents and
would make one of the greatest actresses
the world has known—would, in fact,
succeed in almost any intellectual pursuit—
she feels that this is her mission, and in
doing it she is carrying out the life-work of
her father and of a long line of Christian
ancestors. Her noble aim is to awaken
an interest in the Bibie and by teachings
and explanations with living pictures and
scenes make the word of God so plain, so
simple and so beautiful that every child
will understand and be benefited by it.
We usually expect when we see so large a
woman as Madame Mountford to find in
her a lack of gentleness and much that is
masculine. Not so the Madame. A little
chat with her in her room dispels at
once this illusion and convinces the most
incredulous that she is all gentleness, refine-
ment and altogether charming. Her voice
is low and sweet and her suave and cour-
teous manners are those of an Oriental of
noble blood. She has lectured for fourteen
years, most of the time in foreign lands,
and it is said that her name has become
a familiar household word throughout all
England. She has visited America severel
times and has lectured in most of the large
cities in the East. This is, however, her
first trip to the coast. She went to Salt
Lake City under engagement of the Boston
Concert and Lecture Bereau. Her season
with them having expired she came to Cali-
fornia- not on a lecturing tour, but simply
to see the country of which she had heard
so much. During the Woman’s Congress
in San Francisco she was persuaded to
give a series of lectures and having met
with unqualified success there and here she
has concluded to visit all the larger cities
in the State, and will lecture in them be-
ginning this week in Sacramento. Dr.
Ellis will act as her agent while in this
State. To one comfortably seated in the
audience and gazing upon scene after scene
of Oriental splendor, made so intensly
interesting and beautiful by this gifted wo-
man, but little idea can be formed of the
vast amount of work that goes on behind
the scenes. After having closed, as she
thought, her lecturing tour for the season
she sent her four trained assistants back to
England and came here with only her
maid and valet. As it requires from four-
teen to twenty men, women and children to
assist her in illustrating her lectures, it is

[Column 2]
no easy matter to rehearse and train such a
large number for these lectures. I was an
interested spectator behind the scenes and
watched her as she labored unceasingly
with them up to the last moment before the
curtain rose. Although her maid and valet
rendered some assistance in converting her
company into priests, scribes, shepherds,
Samaritans, Bedouins etc., yet all of the
draping of veils and sashes, sorting out and
arranging of robes, the winding of yards
and yards of material which she rolls into
ropes and puts around and around the
heads in turban fashion, the putting on of
jewels, necklaces and armlets and a thou-
sand and one other details are all done by
the artistic and graceful touch of the
Madame herself. So by the time all receive
the last touch, which is to give the rosy
tint to the cheeks and lips and the dark
shade to the eyebrows and eyes, any other
woman would have been too exhausted to
have gone upon the stage at all, much less
to have held the undivided attention of a
large audience for three hours, but not so
Madame Mountford. Her power of endur-
ance seemed to be equal to her intellect.
When she lectures in the evening she eats
nothing after her noonday meal until her
lecture is over. If she is to give a matinee
no food or nourishment of any kind is taken
after her early breakfast until the matinee
is over, and then very little if she is to
lecture on the same evening. The Madame
has expended $25,000 on the robes,
draperies and Oriental paraphernalia which
she carries with her. They are of heavy
linen, fine camels-hair and silks, most of
which are heavily embroidered and inter-
woven with gold, all done by the hands of
the Oriental women of her country.


POLITICAL SCIENCE STUDY.

	IN the third year’s work of its course of
study in Political Science, the National
American Woman Suffrage Association in-
vites its constituency and its friends, to
unite in earnest investigation and discus-
sion of the more prominent social problems
which are demanding public attention at
this time. It is itself a non-partisan reform
body, representing only one proposed
change. In no sense does it espouse any of
the solutions proposed for the social prob-
lems to which it invites attention. Its only
object in inaugurating the course of study is
to disseminate political information, and its
only hope is. that there shall develop, on
the part of its students and their friends, a
truer reform spirit, a broader tolerance, a
warmer hospitality to new ideas, and a
clearer sense of the duty each individual
owes to the future.
	The Announcement contains a list of
books required for the year, and complete
directions for their use. It may be obtained
free by addressing Carrie Chapman Catt,
107 World’s Building, N. Y.


A RULE of the Postoffice Department for-
bids publications to be sent to persons in
lieu of dues paid to an organization. For
this reason the National Suffrage Bulletin
can no longer be sent to the dollar members
of the National Association. The Septem-
ber number will go to all as usual, but they
will be marked “sample copies,” and any
member who wishes to continue the Bulle-
tin must send the subscription price, 25
cents.

SALT LAKE CITY, OCTOBER 15, 1897.


[Column 1]
MUNICIPAL AFFAIRS.

	IF to give expression to such sublime
sentiments as those uttered by President
Woodruff at the late Conference be deemed
treason, then has the world gone backward
instead of forward. If to declare honesty
is before partisanship is wrong, then have
we indeed come upon perilous times, that
must try honest men's souls to the utter-
most. Surely women have not yet become
so wedded to parties that they must vote
with a certain party, whether their own
conscience will approve or not. If the wo-
men of this city will heed the signs of the
times, and notice how vice and corruption
have grown and flourished in this fair land
within the last few years, there need be no
fear of misrule, for if all honorable upright
men, vote for men of principle and strict
integrity and the great majority of women
join with them in the struggle for a change
in city government, there will be no cause
for fear of being outdone by any party poli-
ticians, who are after the spoils of office.
	The words of wise and good men like
President Woodruff should be heeded in
this campaign, and wisdom and prudence
should be exercised in urging upon those
who are not informed of the conditions of
the city government, that they may know
of the traps and snares laid for the unsuspect-
ing and unwary. It is not the time for
silence but the people should be fearless and
willing to speak the truth and stand for the
right. This spirit of resistance against
those who oppress the taxpayer and the
laborer is not confined to Salt Lake City
nor to Utah, although if it were the cause
is just, but in several large cities of the
East, the non partisan theory has been put
in active operation.
	In one of the leading magazines of the
day the writer upon this subject speaking
from experience says: “With me associ-
ated as treasurer of the board was Mr.
Avery E. Andrews. He was a Democrat,
I a Republican, and there were questions of
of national politics on which we disagreed
widely; but such questions could not enter
into the administration of the New York
police if that administration was to be both
honest and efficient, and as a matter of fact,
during my two years service Mr. Andrews
and I worked in absolute harmony on every
important question of policy which arose.
The prevention of blackmail and corruption,
the suppression of crime and violence, the
safe guarding of life and property, securing
honest elections and rewarding efficient and 

[Column 2]
punishing inefficient police service, are not
and cannot be properly made questions of
party difference. In other words such a
body as the New York police force can be
wisely and properly administered only up-
on a non-partisan basis, and both Mr. 
Andrews and myself were quite incapable
of managing it on any other.
	“Certain of the difficulties we had to face
were merely those which confronted the en-
tire administration in its management of the
municipality. Many worthy people expect-
ed that this reform administration would
make an absolute revolution, not merely in
the government but in the minds of the
citizens as a whole; and felt vaguely that
they had been cheated because there was
not an immediate changing of every bad
influence in social life.
	“In administering the police force we
found, as might be expected, that there was no
need of genius, nor indeed of any very un-
usual qualities. What was required was
the exercise of the plain ordinary virtues,
of a rather commonplace type, which all
good citizens should be expected to possess.
Common sense, common honesty, courage,
energy, resolution, readiness to learn, and a
desire to be as pleasant with everybody as
was compatible with a strict performance
of duty: these were the qualities most call-
ed for.
	“Our methods for restoring order and
discipline were simple, and hardly less so
were our methods for securing efficiency.
	“It was not at all difficult to protect
decent people in their rights, and this re-
sult was effected at once. It was the en-
forcement of the liquor law which caused
most excitement.”
	On this subject the writer has much to
say and closes it after several columns by
saying: “The result of our labors
was of value to the city, for we gave the
citizens better protection than they had
ever before received, and at the same time
root out the corruption which was eating
away civic morality. We were attacked
with the most bitter animosity by many
sensational newspapers and every politician
of the baser sort, not because of our short-
comings, but because of what we did that
was good. We enforced the laws as they
were on the statute books, we broke up
blackmail, we kept down the spirit of dis-
order and suppressed rascality, and we ad-
ministered the force with an eye single to
the welfare of the city. In doing this we
encountered, as we had expected, venom-
ous opposition of all men to whose interest
it was that corruption should continue, or
who were of such dull morality that they
were not willing to see honesty triumph at
the cost of strife.”
	“We were obliged to treat all questions that
arise purely on their merits, without refer-
ence to the desires of the politician. We
went into this course with our eyes open,
for we knew the trouble it would cause us
personally, and what was far more import-
ant, the way in which our efforts for re-
form would consequently be hampered.
However, there was no alternative but to
abide the result. We had counted the cost
before we adopted our plan, and we follow-
ed it resolutely to the end.”
	Some of the best women in New York
City came forward in the movement made
in 1894 spoken of here, and lent their sup- 
port in various ways, though they had not
the ballot as women have in Utah. If
effective work can be done without the fran-

[Column 3]
chise, think how much more weight women
can have who hold the voting power. It
seems quite essential that the women of this
city at the present time should inform
themselves concerning this important ques-
tion of non-partisanship in municipal affairs
and vote understandingly, which is easy
enough to do if one will take a little time to
study the questions. It is simply a duty
which the women of this city owe to them-
selves and the community of which they
form a part, to stand resolutely and deter-
mindly for good government by a faithful
administration of the laws upon the statute
books. Surely no woman with any pro-
nounced ideas of honesty, economy and
morality would wish to see the present
conditions continue. A radical change
must come, and the only course to pursue
to avoid strife and trouble, is to declare for a
non-partisan administration of municipal
affairs.


MISS BELLE KEARNEY.

	MISS BELLE KEARNEY, a celebrated
teacher and lecturer and a great favorite
with the public wherever she has spoken
both in America and across the ocean,
recently visited Salt Lake City and spoke
in the large Tabernacle, Saturday evening,
Oct. 2. Her lecture was entitled “Why
Are The Wheels Clogged?’’and here as else-
where, she was received with great favor.
Miss Kearney is traveling under the auspices
of the Woman’s Christian Temperance
Union. Miss Frances E. Willard speaks of
her eloquence and devotion to the cause in
the highest terms of praise. Miss Kearney
came to Utah partly to attend a Conven-
tion of the W. C. T. U. held here at that
time, and she made several speeches at the
Convention that were highly complimented.
She is a young Southerner from Mississippi
with the pleasing Southern accent and an
easy, graceful manner.
	President Angus M. Cannon presided
during the evening in the Tabernacle, and
introduced Miss Kearney, and Elder A. E.
Miner offered prayer. The Committee had
succeeding in securing some of the best
musical talent in the city for a musical pro-
gram. Prof. J. J. McLellan rendered in
his inimitable style, with great feeling and
expression the “Ave Marie” from Mascag-
nis’ opera Cavelleira Rusticana, and Mrs.
Lizzie Thomas Edwards sang “Just As I
Am” by Sudds, with intense feeling, and
her high notes rang out clear and distinct,
which is always a pleasing feature in vocal
music. Prof. Heber S. Goddard sang an
Aria by Donizetti, his magnificent voice
filling the large building with rich vocal
tones, awakening tender and sublime emo-
tions. A sacred thing indeed is the gift of
song, and accompanied by such music as
Prof. McLellan is capable of rendering,
and in a building that is of itself lofty and
inspiring to the soul, one cannot help feel-
ing it must be a joy to give so much pleas-
ure to others.
	Miss Kearney is a very fluent and origin-
al speaker, she uses no notes, nor is she in
any sense sensational. Her voice is far-
reaching and full of pathos, and her tones
are never monotonous. She is a young wo-
man of strong character and a just desire to
do good, she is giving her talents and heart
to this missionary work.
	A reception was tendered Miss Kearney
at the residence of Mrs. Annie T. Hyde


where those who were anxious to meet her
socially might have the opportunity. Mrs.
Hyde’s handsome parlors were artistically
decorated with beautiful autumn leaves and
boughs, and presented an almost fairy-like
appearance, and with the cordial welcome
of the pleasing hostess and her sweet man-
nered and amiable daughters, the afternoon
was very enjoyable, even though the storm
kept many of the ladies from calling.
About thirty, however, availed themselves
of the privilege of meeting and chatting for
a few moments with Miss Kearney, who is
a delightful conversationalist in a social
way, as well as a wonderful public speaker.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
BIRTHDAY ENTERTAINMENT.

	THE Central Board of the Primary Asso-
ciations, Mrs. Louie B. Felt president,
planned a very excellent entertainment in
honor of Mrs. Aurelia S. Rogers to be held
in the Fourteenth ward Assembly Hall on
the evening of her birthday, October 4, in
this city. Preparations had been going on
for some time previous and the affair proved
a perfect success.
	Mrs. Rogers is known throughout Zion as
the originator of the Primary Associations,
which have been such a blessing and bene-
fit not only to the children, but to many of
the parents; whatever is a spiritual or a
moral uplifting is a blessing to the world,
and this Association reaches to the little
ones, or should do, in each ward in every
Stake of Zion.
	The hall was beautifully and tastefully
decorated in every available place with
flowers and Autumn leaves and boughs
which gave a charming effect, and the
platform and tables looked very attractive.
Mrs. Felt, her counselor, Mrs. Lillie T.
Freeze and her secretary, Miss May Ander-
son and others associated with them, receiv-
ed the guests and managed the program in
a very creditable manner. Where there
were so many things that deserved men-
tion it is rather difficult to select, but the
delightful paper prepared by Mrs. Lillie T.
Freeze and read by Mrs. Maggie Bassett
certainly should be favorably noticed. It
was a history of the work in brief giving
honor where honor was due, and was writ-
ten in a very pleasing style and read in a
way that all could appreciate. The paper
will, of course, be published and preserved
on record. Mrs. Lucy A. Clark had written
a sweet song which was sung during the
evening and was one of the pleasing fea-
tures of the entertainment.
	Mrs. Rogers, the recipient of the honors,
was a picture and a study. It was pleasant
to look at her as she partly reclined in her 
easy chair (for she came from a sick bed to
be present,) she was most becomingly attir- 
ed and her face always serene in repose was
an index to the soul within, pure and spirit-
ual. She received and responded to the
congratulations showered upon her with
that ease of manner characteristic of the
Spencers. That she was surprised when
she learned all that had been done was ap-
parent, yet there was no outburst, nor
special demonstration.
	The children who took part in the pro-
gram (and it would not have been complete
without children) rendered their exercises
in a very satisfactory and delightful way,
and the best of selections had been made.
	Mrs. Rogers had for some time been de-
sirous of publishing her life and also the
origin, development and progress of the

[Column 2]
Primary Associations, and had collected to-
gether the material and written the manu-
script, and the Central Board, under the
management of its president, Mrs Felt,
have raised the means to secure the pub-
lication desired. Elder Joseph Felt an-
nounced the fact to the audience by reading
a letter from the publishers, Geo. Q. Can-
non Sons and Co. This is the most fitting
tribute that could be bestowed by the Asso-
ciation upon one whose inspired thought
brought about such spiritual growth in the
little ones, the children of the Saints.
	Appropriate remarks were made, songs
were rendered and gifts of lovely flowers
were given and delicious refreshments were
served by beautiful young girls, and Presi-
dent Felt and her associates must have
been highly gratified by the good spirit and
feeling that was manifest in all that was
said and done.


[Column 1]
A RINGING ADDDRSS.

To the People of Salt Lake City:
	EVERY taxpayer is an investor, every
resident a shareholder in the government of
his city. All are directly interested in its
prosperity and advancement. Shortsighted
and selfish is he who makes any other in-
terest paramount to the public welfare;
desperate is the condition of any municipal-
ity where cliques or rings or incompetence
or partisanship control in official affairs,
and business methods become subordinate
to personal or political advancement. In-
dustry languishes, extravagance runs riot,
patriotism is repressed, the honest pride of
citizens dwindles and the people suffer.
	We hold it to be necessary to state, re-
gretfully yet plainly, certain facts with
reference to the condition of public affairs in
Salt Lake City. It is better to tell the truth
now, even at the cost of advertising our
misfortunes, than by concealment and in-
activity to rush swiftly to further sufferings.
	Heavy debts, increase of offices, large
salaries and consequent excessive taxation
have made the burdens upon the people un-
bearable. In 1890 the aggregate of taxa-
tion in Salt Lake City was eleven mills; in
1897 it is twenty seven and one half mills.
Even this enormous figure will not cover
current expenses at their present volume;
and when to this is added that the valua-
tion on which the assessment is made is in
many instances greater than the property
is worth, there will be hone to dispute that
Salt Lake City bears a burden heavier than
almost any city in the land. Depreciation
in values since 1890 has gone hand in hand
with increase in expenses; the ease with
which public money has been expended is
only to be compared with the difficulty the
people have had in supplying it. All things
considered, the statement is fairly suscepti-
ble of proof that taxes have increased more
than two hundred per cent during the seven
years, while the prevailing depression has
many times multiplied the difficulties of the
taxpayer. The load thus placed upon the
small wage-earner and home-owner has
been and is grievous to be borne; among
the poorer classes it has in many cases
amounted to virtual confiscation. Public
improvements which under a more conserv-
ative policy might have been prosecuted,
and thus furnished employment to many
people, have had to be postponed. Nor
have the poorer classes been the only suf-
ferers.  Investments have been discouraged

[Column 2]
because property, even where favorably-
situated, is able to return little more than 
the amount of the taxes; capital, naturally
timid, has not looked this way with favor;
and business men generally have felt to the
utmost extent the voracity of the tax mons-
ter which consumed so much of the profits
of their enterprise and toil.
	 For the present year the city’s income
has been partially anticipated and used—a
deficit exists, in the payment of which a
large portion of the current year’s taxes will
be needed that should properly go only for
next year’s necessary expenditures. In
addition to ordinary expenses, fifty thou-
sand dollars per year, commencing with
1898, must be raised as a sinking fund for
the payment of our municipal debt, which,
as a fitting climax to these financial woes,
may be announced as reaching the enorm-
ous sum of over $2,509,000. Besides all
other evils that come from this condition of
public affairs, the city is prevented from
doing necessary work in preserving and
adding to municipal improvements, the con-
sequence of which is that the city’s progress
is stopped.
	This is a gloomy yet a truthful picture.
Happily we believe a remedy and relief can
be had. The situation calls for the highest
patriotism; men of undoubted integrity and
business ability must be called to office, re-
gardless of politics and every other consider-
ation save the capacity for the duties re-
quired.
	We affirm it as a correct principle and
one that should prevail henceforth, that the
government Of this city should be taken per-
manently out of politics and put wholly on
a business basis. Party administration in
municipal affairs should be forever abolish-
ed. This is no experiment, no new doc-
trine. The best governed cities of the Old
World have made it triumphant, the larg-
est and most progressive American cities
are struggling successfully with the same
problem. The hand of the politician will
in the near future be shaken off in all muni-
cipal affairs, party polities in municipal
campaigns will have no place. Salt Lake
City should be in the front in this great re-
form. Its business should be managed by
business men of approved fitness and ex-
perience. Its servants and employees should
be selected for these qualifications and no
others, just as any private or corporate
business, of which the municipality is after
all but the larger counterpart. Every
shareholder has the right to demand this in
the one case as well as in the other; the
highest success and the best results can be
achieved in no other way.
	Therefore, we who have signed our
names to this address, affirm and hold to
these principles and declarations:
	That party politics have no necessary
connection with municipal management:
	That only such persons should be select-
ed for municipal office as are honest, effici-
ent and willing to devote the requisite time
to the public service, having no unneces-
sary deputies or assistants:
	Who will conduct public sffairs on econ-
omical and strictly business principles:
	Who will levy no higher taxes than are
absolutely needed for public expenses:
	Who will reduce salaries to figures con-
sonant with financial conditions, paying
only such compensation for public services
as would be paid in private business:
	Who will treat public trust as an honor
conferred, not an opportunity for privat gain.


We cordially invite the people of this
city of all parties, creeds and opinions to
join with us in this effort to establish an
efficient, economical, non-partisan munici-
pal government. In furtherance of this
effort we place in nomination the following
named persons to fill the various elective
municipal offices, to be voted for at the
coming election, pledging them our sup-
port and appealing to all patriotic citizens
to do likewise:
CITIZENS REFORM TICKET.
	Mayor—JOHN CLARK.
	Recorder—MISS JOAN SPRAGUE. 
	Treasurer—JOSEPH E. CAINE.
	Auditor—GEORGE SWAN.
	Attorney—PARLEY L. WILLIAMS.
	Police Justice—C. A. WOOLLEY.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.
	MINUTES of the Ladies’ Semi-Monthly
Meeting held in the 14th Ward Hall,
Saturday, Oct. 2, 1897, President M. I. 
Horne presiding. Singing “Come, come
ye Saints.” Prayer by Sister Wright. Sing-
ing “Let those who would be Saints in-
deed.”
	President Horne was very sorry to see so
few here this afternoon but thought it was
owing to the near approach of conference.
Felt there were enough for a blessing and
said this was a trying time for the Saints,
that when we first came here to the valley
that was a trying time but the present time
was the more so. Now was the time to be
tested; now was the time to catechise our-
selves and look into our own hearts. Spoke
of the way we were being tried and perse-
cuted by extremely high taxes. She was
surprised that some of our people would
quarrel and wrangle over politics. We 
should ask our Heavenly Father which
course to take and trust to Him, and to go
to Him humbly and He would hear us.
Spoke in favor of the Non-Partisan ticket, 
said for years she had been in favor of hav-
ing a party that was non-partisan, and said
President D. H. Wells said that the women
were a power in the land. Made some ex-
cellent remarks on this subject. Felt if we
all who were here, though few, testified to
the goodness of God we would be blest.
	The minutes having arrived were read
and accepted.
	Sister Lydia D. Alder felt that President
Horne had dwelt on many precious subjects
especially that of praying for light on this
coming election. Spoke beautifully on
prayer, and said many times the feeling of
prayer would come over us and at the time
we might be busy, still she felt that the
thought ascended up to heaven and was
accepted. Spoke of the efficacy of prayer
in the case of President Woodruff’s daugh-
ter. Said many times our prayers were not
answered but when this was the case the
Lord would show us that it was better for
us not to have what we had prayed for.
Urged the sisters to fully trust Him, and
said nothing that comes to us would harm
us but would turn to blessings on our
heads. Referred to the vision that Joseph
and Hyrum Smith had when they saw their
mother home praying for them, at the time
when they had the cholera.
	Sister M. O. Brown spoke on prayer, said
she loved to pray and thought nothing
brought to her the comfort that prayer did.
Spoke on asking counselfrom those in
authority.

[Column 2]
	Sister E. J. Stevenson said she had
enjoyed the prayer of Sister Wright which
was a sermon in itself, also the remarks of
our President. She had only just got
home from a trip in the country. Gave a
very interesting account of her trip in the
interest of the Relief Society work. Urged
the sisters to be humble and prayerful and
to be true to one another, thought it was
dreadful to have hard feelings towards any
one, but if such were the case it was our
duty to go to the person, or persons, and
make it right. Spoke of the duty of
mothers and urged the sisters to bear their
testimony.
	Sister Wright felt we should let nothing
come along to prevent us from coming to
these meetings. When she was a little
girl her father taught her to pray, and used
to tell her to never let the sun get down on
her anger for she might never see it again.
Spoke of a recent conversation she had
with a lady stranger, felt she had done
some little good in doing so. Rejoiced in
her Temple work and prayed she might
always remain faithful.
	Sister E. Webb felt Sister Wright had
given us very good advice about leaving
our household cares and attending our
meetings. Said many times she had left
dinner dishes as well as the wash tub to
attend her meeting because she felt it was
her duty. Felt sorry that there was so
much strife and division in politics as there
is, and spoke of the happy time we used to
enjoy when this was not the case. Spoke
on prayer and said there was nothing that
gave such comfort as going off by ourselves
and asking our Heavenly Father to help
us; also spoke of the anxiety that mothers
felt for their children and of the joy her
Temple work brought to her, said it help-
ed her to bear the burdens of life, for we all
had them. Urged the sisters to come here.
	Sister Mary L. Morris felt she had been
blest in coming here this afternoon, many
precious things had been spoken. Made
some very pleasing remarks.
	Sister Lizzie Decker said she felt she
must speak of the goodness of God to her,
and said she never could have lived if it
had not been that she could go to the Lord
and ask Him for help. Referred to her
Temple work and the benefit it had been to
her, also spoke of her anxiety for her child-
ren and asked the blessing of Heaven upon
us.
	Sister Frances Smith spoke of the faith
of her little grand-daughter who was very
much afflicted with scarlet fever, she said
she knew if they would send her name to
her Sunday school to be prayed for she
would be healed. This was done and she
was healed almost instantly.
	Testimonies were borne by Sisters Sarah
Phelps and Alice Woolley. President Horne
made some excellent closing remarks and
meeting was adjourned for two weeks.
	Singing ‘‘From all that dwell below.”
Benediction by Sister Lula G. Richards.
				ZINA H. BULL, Sec’y.


U.W. P. C.

	MINUTES of the U. W. P. C. held
Sep. 30. President Fox called the meeting
to order at the usual time. After prayer by
Mrs. Stevenson, the secretary called the
roll, members responding with sentiments
from Elizabeth Barrett Browning.
	Under “unfinished business” the amend-
ments to the Federation Constitution by the


Woman’s club, which was laid on the table
at the previous meeting, were brought be-
fore the club. Amendment to Art. III,
sec. 3 was read and discussed. A motion
was made by Dr. Ferguson that the amend-
ment be accepted The motion was lost.
The amendment to the by laws, Art. II.
was read and discussed. Motion by Dr.
Ferguson that this amendment be accepted.
Carried. The club voted that the annual
business meeting should be held Monday,
October 18, at 11 a. m.
	Next in order was the critic’s report by
Dr. Shipp. Dr. Pratt was appointed critic
for the evening.
	The evening’s program was as follows:
Topic—“Alaska,” Mrs. Aimee Schiller.
Original poem—“The Woman Preacher,”
Dr. Shipp. Mrs. Dickinson was unable to
be present and her paper on the “Effect of
Klondike gold discoveries on American in-
terests” was postponed.
	Mrs. Lawler at the request, of the club
described the vessel in which she crossed
the Atlantic, also a number of points and
places of interest in Paris. The program
for the October meeting was discussed and
suggestions made. A motion was made
and carried that at the October meeting
each member represent some prominent
character in fiction. A motion was made
and carried that each member be permitted
to bring an escort. Dr. Shipp graciously
offered her home for that meeting. The
club decided to have refreshments and
Pearl Russell, Mrs. Lovesy, Mrs. Wilcox,
Mrs. Hyde and Clara Ferguson were ap-
pointed as a committee to assist Dr. Shipp.
Dr. Pratt gave her report as critic, the
minutes were read and after benediction by
Mrs. Young the club adjourned for one
month.
			ORIELLE CURTIS, Sec.


R. S. AND P. A. REPORTS.

WAYNE STAKE.
	Relief Society Conference held at Canes-
ville August 24, 1897 in the afternoon,
President Jane S. Coleman presiding. Sing-
ing “How firm a foundation.” Prayer by
Patriarch F. W. Young. Singing “What
was witnessed in the heavens.”
	Prasident. Jane S. Coleman said it afford-
ed her much pleasure to meet with the
brethren and sisters in Relief Society con-
ference. She had lately visited the local
branches of the Relief Society in the west-
ern part of the Stake, all were feeling and
doing well and were energetic in their
labors. Encouraged the sisters to attend
their meetings, especially the monthly testi-
mony meetings, and advised the young sis-
ters to attend also, that a bond of friendship
and good feeling should exist between the
Y. L. M. I. A. and the Relief Society by
mingling together; good advice can be
given, virtue taught and much good
accomplished. Spoke of looking after the
poor and the necessity for the members to
give temporal aid (donations) that the presi-
dent may have the necessary means at
hand to carry out what is required of her.
Wished the sisters to be more interested in
the EXPONENT by subscribing for it.
	Sister Eliza Rust, president of the Canes-
ville Relief Society, gave a good report.
Said all the young sisters had joined and
were members of the Relief Society. They
have a lot nicely fenced and cultivated,
they got a good crop of beans on part of it.
	President Mary A. White reported Giles


[Column 1]
in good condition. Sister Robinson (our
Stake President’s mother) said she had been
interested in the remarks made. She had
visited the Relief Society with Sister Cole-
man and had an enjoyable time. Spoke
upon prayer and the testimony of the Gos-
pel, also upon obeying the Word of Wis-
dom, and gave good advice.
	Sister Mary Ballard reported the Thur-
ber Relief Society in good running order.
Said they were building a Relief Society
hall and had very good success in their
undertaking.
	Counselor Mary E. Hanks spoke well,
encourged the mothers to attend their meet-
ings and set an example for their daughters,
also spoke upon virtue, the Word of Wis-
dom and keeping the commandments of
God.
	President H. M. Hansen felt pleased to
hear the good reports of the sisters and
encouraged them to continue to press for-
ward, and their efforts to accomplish good
would be crowned with success.
	President G. Bastian said he had been
interested in the remarks made, also in
hearing the reports given. Spoke on being
kind to the poor, that the needy should not
think or feel bad to receive from the Relief
Society when in need of assistance. He
spoke to the young, said to be kind to their
parents.
	Bishop Hanks had been interested in the
good remarks during the conference and
appreciated the labors of the Relief Society.
Spoke on prayer and a virtuous life.
	President W. E. Robinson said he felt to
endorse the remarks of his brethren and sis-
ters and was pleased with the reports of the
different branches of the Society. He
always speaks very encouragingly to the
sisters and sustains them by attending the
conferences and giving a word in season on
many good subjects. Conference adjourned
till called. Singing. Benediction by
Elder Joseph Eckersley.
			ANNA COLEMAN, Sec.
BEAVER STAKE.
	THE Relief Society of Beaver Stake held
its conference in the Society Hall, Beaver
City, Utah, Tuesday Sep. 3, 1897, com-
mencing at 10 o’clock a. m. Present on
the stand were President C. D. White,
Counselors M. L. Shepherd and J. F. Tol-
ton, Bishop Geo. Mumford, Patriarch D.
Tyler, Prof. W. G. Bickley, Sisters C. C.
Shepherd, S. M. Dell, President J. W.
Bickley and others. Meeting was called to
order by Sister C. C. Shepherd. Singing
“Hail to the brightness.” Prayer by
Patriarch D. Tyler. Singing “Praise to
the man.” Minutes of previous meeting
read and approved.
	Sister Shepherd felt thankful to see so
many present, still there is a vacancy in
our midst, but we know that our dear
departed President, Sister Tyler, did her
work and did it well. Felt sorry that
through sickness Sister M. A. White was
prevented from being with us. She had
in company with other sisters visited
through the Stake and found a good set of
sisters, all alive to their duties.
	President J. W. Bickley reported the
Beaver Relief Society in good condition and
working unitedly. Invited all to attend
the dedicatory services of the new granery
on the 16th inst.
	Sister E. E. Owen said there was a good
spirit here, and contrasted the condition of
the Saints who own comfortable homes as

[Column 2]
being far in advance of a large portion of
the world.
	President C. D. White felt pleased to see
so many present, no ward is complete with
out a Relief Society. The women of a com-
munity shape its destiny. Said he was very
sorry to have to reorganize the Stake Soci-
ety, and spoke very highly of our departed
president. Did not know whom the sisters
would like for president, desired each one
to write a first and second choice on slips
of paper, and himself and counselors would
look them over during the noon hour and
decide. Made many encouraging and
instructive remarks, and said this was a
good year to store grain.
	Bishop Geo. Mumford said the duties of
the sisters as well as the brethren are great.
There is a place for every one in this
Church, let our Church duties be first.
Spoke on economy, and said we should
give kind advice to our young people. If
we see any a little wayward take them
aside and talk kindly and show them their
danger.
	Counselor M. A. Tolton bore a faithful
testimony to the Gospel and said we
should sustain our officers and the author-
ties. Meeting adjourned till 2 p. m. Sing-
ing “Be it my only wisdom here.” Bene-
diction by Counselor M. L. Shepherd.
	AFTERNOON SESSION:—Meeting called
to order at 2 p.m. by Sister C. C. Shepherd.
Singing “Glory to God on high.” prayer
by Prof. W. C. Bickley. Singing “I saw
a mighty angel fly.” The secretary read
a letter from Sister Hannah Sorenson, also
one from Dr. Allen of Provo in regard to
Sister Sorenson’s class of work. Sister L.
W. Jones explained why we should have
those lectures.
	Sister S. M. Dell bore her testimony and
desired to be useful. Counselor M L.
Shepherd said the duties of the Relief Soci-
ety were many and varied. Spoke on
economy, cleanliness, training of our child-
ren and the storing of grain.
	Sister Lydia White made a few excellent
remarks, wanted to do all the good she
could. Sister M. E. Murdock spoke very
highly of Sister Sorenson and hoped we
would get her here and be benefited by her
good instructions. Spoke against slander.
	Counselor J. F. Tolton praised the
labors of the Relief Society and the interest
taken therein. Unity brings success. No
unclean spirit can enjoy the benefits derived
from the Gospel. Sister Mary Fernley and
Betsy Goodwin made excellent remarks.
Patriarch D. Tyler spoke of the ability and
talent of Sister Sorenson. Said we want to
advance in knowledge.
	The biographical sketch and resolutions
formulated by the Beaver Relief Society in
behalf of our beloved President, Sister Ruth
W. Tyler, printed in Vol. 25, No. 22 of the
Woman’s Exponent were read by Sister
L. W. Jones and adopted by the Stake
Relief Society.
	Sister Alice M. T. Tanner urged the
sisters to sustain the EXPONENT. Presi-
dent C. D. White spoke very emphatically
in regard to sustaining the EXPONENT,
also spoke highly in regard to Sister Soren-
son’s labor. “We need such instructions.
The sisters have been unanimous in select-
ing Sister Cedaressa C. Shepherd for presi-
dent and we are well pleased with the
choice.” She was sustained and set apart,
but being surprised, was unprepared to
select counselors. Alice M. T. Tanner as
secretary and Emily C. Olsen as treasurer

[Column 3]
were set apart for those offices. President
Shepherd made few closing remarks and
conference adjourned for three months. 
Singing “May the grace of Christ our
Savior.” Benediction by Bishop George
Mumford.
		ALICE M. T. TANNER, Sec.
	P. S. Sister Shepherd having selected
her counselors they were set apart at the
Beaver Stake quarterly conference, Sunday 
Sep. 19, 1897, as follows: First counselor,
Mary A. White; second counselor, Harriet
E. Fotheringham.
MEADOW PRIMARY.
Editor Woman's Exponent:
	WE thought perhaps you would like to
hear from the Meadow Primary Association
which was reorganized August 9th, 1892,
with Martha B. Adams president; Isabella
Stott first and Elizabeth B. Bennett second
counselors; Alice J. Duncan secretary, and
Mary H. Adams assistant secretary. There
are about one hundred names enrolled; it is
mostly kept up by the smaller children.
	We hold meetings every Friday after-
noon. The Primary is averagely attended.
We had a Primary entertainment Wednes-
day evening, August 11, for the purpose of
obtaining means to help publish a book
written by Aurelia Spencer Rogers. The
program was as follows: Singing by the
Primary choir entitled “The Primary pray-
er song.” Prayer by Counselor N. M.
Stewart. Singing “Joseph Smith’s first
prayer.” An opening speech by Elizabeth
B. Bennett. Articles of Faith. Song by
Amy and Ethel Bushnell, “I’m not too
young for God to see.” Sentiments by
Lilas Adams, Myrtle Stewart and Ornald
Beckstrand. Music by Mandolin and 
Guitar Club, which played between the
other parts being taken. Bible questions
and answers. Recitation by Amy Stott,
“Angel Guides.” A Swedish song by
Sigred and Emma Pearson. Acrostic reci- 
tation by twenty little girls. A recitation
entitled “John Taylor” by ten little boys.
The Primary Throng by twelve girls. A
cane drill by twenty-five boys. Singing
“Merry, merry children sweetly sing.”
Prayer by H. G. Labrum Sen. The
admission was five cents each for both
young and old. The entertainment was
well attended, the proceeds were $6.75.
Six of the young ladies of Meadow assisted
us in getting up the program.
			ALICE J. DUNCAN. Sec’y.
SALT LAKE STAKE PRIMARY.
	Minutes of Primary conference of the
Salt Lake Stake held Sep. 25, 1897, at the
Assembly Hall, President Camilla C. Cobb
presiding. On the stand with her were
her counselors, Lydia A. Wells and Mary
L. Morris, President. Zina D. H. Young
and others. Meeting opened by singing
“Gladly Meeting.” Prayer by Sister Emily
Hollings. Singing “Little children love
the Savior.’’ Roll called with thirty-one
associations repreaented, the children re-
sponding with sentiments. Minutes of
previous meeting read and approved.
	President Zina D. H. Young spoke to
the children for a short time. She said she
felt to welcome them to the meeting, it
filled her heart with joy to see them. Re-
lated a story about Joseph Smith hearing
some children praying for him when he was
in danger. He said he knew the Lord


would hear their prayers and his life would
be preserved. She told the children
to never forget their prayers and God would
bless them. She said she had been a
Latter-day Saint for sixty-one years, and
thanked her Heavenly Father for preserv-
ing her life to the present time.
	Clarence Openshaw, from the 16th Ward
gave a recitation. A motion song was
rendered by a number of little girls from
the 11th Ward. Frank Buckwater from
the 3rd ward gave a sketch from the Book
of Mormon. “Hail to the Prophet” was
sung by all the children. A sketch of
early pioneer life in Utah was given by
Ida Peck of Cannon ward. Song by Annie
Peterson of the 12th Ward. Recitation by
Ethel Snow from Draper. Song by Lizzie
Stringfellow from the same place. A drill
was very nicely rendered by ten little girls
from the 15th Ward, west branch associa-
tion. Recitation by Chloe Brown, from
East Draper. One of Eliza R. Snow’s
poems was recited by May Alder, from the
2nd Ward. Myrtle Bagley from the 10th
Ward gave a recitation. Duett by Della
Seddon and Luella Harold from the 5th
Ward.
	Sister Lydia D. Alder expressed her
pleasure in listening to the exercises, and to
see how beautifully the children were
being trained, and said it was a blessed work
to be engaged in. The children would
always remember their Primary teachers.
Announced that the 2nd Ward would have
a Fair during the week and invited all to
visit it.
	Sister Lula G. Richards of the General
board, spoke in regard to Sister Roger’s
book, said she was the founder of the
Primary and hoped they would assist in
publishing her book.
	President Cobb made some closing re-
marks. After singing “In our lovely
Deseret,” benediction was pronounced by
Counselor Joseph E. Taylor.  
					E. W. HYDE, Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


NOTES AND NEWS.

	TWENTY-FIVE Bulgarian women are
studying medicine at the Faculty of Nancy
in France. 
	“WHEN the Women’s College in Luck-
now is finished, it will be the finest educa-
tional building in India.”
	THE campaign in South Dakota was be-
gun in October, with a series of two-day
county conventions, under the auspices of
the National Organization Committee, to be
held in the most important counties.
	MRS. ANNIE M. MACLEAN, a graduate,
of Acadia University, Nova Scotia, took
the degree of Master of Philosophy at the
University of Chicago in July. Miss Mc-
Lean is the first Canadian woman to take a
higher degree from this university. Her
thesis was on ‘‘Factory Legislation for Wo-
men in the United States.”
	A MEDALLION of Miss Anthony, prepared
by the Rochester Club, is nine inches in
diameter, made of delicately-tinted plaster.
The reproduction is excellent, the feature's
clear and accurate, and the medallion an
ornament that would adorn any home.
They may be obtained for $1.50, express
paid, of Mrs. F. N. West, 9 Averil-Place,
Rochester, N. Y.


THE sudden death of Mary Kyle Dallas
of New York, about three weeks ago, was a
shock and a sorrow to a host of friends and
admirers. For many years she was a con-
tributor to the New York Ledger at a large
salary, and also wrote short stories for
Lippincott. Latterly her work has been 
seen in the daily papers, and her essays on
women for the American Press Association
commanded wide attention. Mrs. Dallas
was a member of Sorosis, the vice-presi-
dent of the Woman’s Press Club, and a
prominent member of the “Hidden Hand
Club,” whose purpose is to expose the fake
advertisements which prove a snare and a
delusion to unsuspecting women. Mrs.
Dallas was tall with a beautiful, aristocratic
face and stately bearing, while her manners
were so gracious and her acts so kindly that
she endeared herself to all who knew her.
She was fifty years of age, and began to
write at the age of eight, when she com-
posed a play that was produced in her
father’s kitchen. In 1888 she estimated
that she had written and sold at least eight
thousand stories and as many sketches.


UTAH CHARITY KINDERGARTEN.

	THE entertainment to be given at the
Theatre tonight, Oct. 15, is to be one of
the most unique affairs ever given by the
children of this city. Miss May Earle has
been indefatigable in her labor to make it
a success, and Mrs. H. S. Young the presi-
dent of-the association, and her co-workers
have worked incessantly to secure every
possible advantage to make it telling and
effective. Mrs. Kate Bridewell Anderson
has rendered valuable assistance, and the
little people who are to appear in the oper-
ratta (The Rebellion of the Daisies) have ex-
quisite costumes. The parents of the Child-
ren who are taking part have spared no
pains, trouble or expense to aid in this
charitable enterprise. If the generous pub-
lic will manifest their interest in this philan-
thropic movement to educate the little ones
by patronizing the entertainment on the two
occasions, tonight and Saturday Matinee,
they will be more than repaid in seeing this
new and novel opera. Prof. H. S. God-
dard has kindly consented to apppar for
sweet charity’s sake.


MISS LOMBROSO, the daughter of the
Italian professor has written a volume, the
title of which is “On the Happiness of
Women.”
	THE annual business meeting of the
U. F. W. C. will be held in the parlors of
the Congregational Church, Saturday morn-
ing October 23, at ten o’clock. Election of
officers, amendments to the constitution and
other matters of business will be transacted.

[Column 1]
A NOCTURNE OF RUBINSTEIN.
SELECTED.
I.
WHAT now remains, what now remains but night?
Night hopeless, since the moon is in her grave!
	Late came a glorious light
In one wide flood on spire and field and wave.
	It found a flowing way
To secret places where the dead leaves lay;
	It won the half-hid stream
To shy remembrance of her morning gleam; 
	Then on the sky’s sharp shore
Rolled back, a fading tide, and was no more.
No more on spire and ivied window bright!
	No more on field and wave! 
What now remains, what now remains but night? 
Night hopeless, since the moon is in her grave!

II.
	Dumb waits the dim, broad land,
Like one who hears, yet cannot understand,
	Tidings of grief to come.
The woods and waters, with the winds, are dumb,
	But now a breeze has found
Sorrowful voice, and sobs along the ground:
“Oh the lost light, the last, the best lost light!
	No more on field and wave!”
What now remains, what now remains but night
Night hopeless, since the moon is in her grave!

III.
	Hark, how the wind outswells,
Tempting the woods dark heart till he rebels,
	And shaking his black hair,
Lifts up a cry of passion and despair!
	The groaning branches chafe
Till scarce the small, hushed singing-birds are safe,
	Tossed rocking in the nest,
Like gentle memories in a stormy breast.
A shudder, as good angels passed in flight,
	Trills over field and wave!
What now remains what now remains but night?
Night hopeless, since the moon is in her grave.

IV.
	There falls a mighty hush:
And forth from far recesses fern-scents rush,
	Faint as a waft from years
Long past; they touch in heaven the springs of
	tears.
	In great drops slow and warm,
Breaks all at once the spirit of the storm.
What now remains what now remains but night?
Night grieving, while the moon is in her grave!

V.
Behold! the rain is over: on the wave

[Column 2]
	A new, a flashing light!
	Lo, she arises calm.
The pale, the patient moon, and pours like balm
	Through the wet wood’s wrecked aisle
Her own unutterably tender smile!

There is no calm like that when storm is done;
There is no pleasure keen as pains release;
There is no joy that lies so deep as peace,
No peace so deep as that by struggle won.

Naught now remains, naught now remains but
	night―
Night peaceful, with the moon on field and wave!
				HELEN GRAY CONE.


THE QUEEN’S THANKS TO HER
PEOPLE.

	Her Majesty addressed to her people all
over the world, through the medium of
the Home Secretary, the following expres-
sion of personal gratitude for the sponta-
neous manifestation of loyalty and affection
that marked her progress on June 22, and
which have come to her from all parts of
the world on this great occasion:—
	WINDSOR CASTLE, July 15, 1897.
	“I have frequently expressed my person-
al feelings to my people, and though on
this memorable occasion there have been
many official expressions of my deep sense
of the unbounded loyalty evinced, I cannot
rest satisfied without personally giving
utterance to these sentiments.
	“It is difficult for me on this occasion to
say how truly touched and grateful I am
for the spontaneous outburst of loyal attach-
ment and real affection which I have exper-
enced on the completion of the Sixtieth
year of my Reign.
	“During my progress through London
on June 22, this great enthusiasm was
shown in the most striking manner, and
can never be effaced from my heart.
	"It is indeed deeply gratifying, after so
many years of labor and anxiety for the
good of my beloved country, to find that
my exertions have been appreciated
throughout my vast Empire.
	“In weal and woe I have ever had the
true sympathy of all my people, which has
been warmly reciprocated by myself.
	“It has given me unbounded pleasure to
see so many of my subjects from all parts
of the world assembled here, and to find
them joining in the acclamations of loyal
devotion to myself, and I would wish to
thank them all from the depth of my grate-
ful heart.
	“I shall ever pray God to bless them and
to enable me still to discharge my duties for
their welfare as long as life lasts.
	“VICTORIA, R. I.”
—Englishwoman’s Review.


NOT only are women taking an increas-
ingly active interest in municipal matters,
but their activity is almost all in favor of
better conditions and greater cleanliness,
literal and metaphorical.		A. S. B.


DR. BARNEY’S TRAVELS IN
COLORADO.

	WEDNESDAY, July 28, early in the fore-
noon I left Salt Lake City in company with
my brother, Carlos Stevens, and nephew,
Walter C. Howell, we arrived Thursday 29
at Hooper, Colorado, after riding five and a
half miles by team, came to the residence of
the family of Mrs. Brown, my niece, after
a happy greeting retired to rest for the
night.
	The few following days were fully occu-
pied in considering the many unwritten
chapters of our lives and in anticipation of
some of the coming events, and in viewing
of the large fields of 160 to 320 acres now
being harvested, mostly of oats and wheat
six and eight feet high, yielding from
twenty-five to forty bushels per acre. This
valley I visited two years ago, and the re-
sult of that visit has brought a very happy-
fying reflection, even to the probability of
others of my kindred immigrating. August
7, left in company with my brother and
after traveling thirty miles to Monte Vista,
Rio Grande Co., staid over night with my
nephew, B. F. Stevens. Then after a long
day ’s travel over a very stony ridge of about
thirty miles, arrived at Richmond, haying
passed Lajara (pronounced Lahara, a small
town situated on the railroad) here we were
kindly entertained over night by Bishop
Coombs. The rough road necessitated our
having a couple of shoes put on our horse
which the Bishop did himself. Then after
traveling six miles further arrived at Man-
assa, stopping at President Alma R. Smith’s
(son of Silas) found Apostle John W.
Taylor seated in council with a company of
Elders who were reporting their visits and
receiving additional daily appointments he
took me by the arm and introduced the to
them as one of the best sisters in the Church.
I felt a little out of place with the prefix,
but like many other agreeable things it was
easily passed by, but this was supplement-
ed with a note signed by John W. Taylor
after arriving at Sister Haskell’s while eat-
ing dinner, that he wanted me to meet
promptly at 5:30 that day, Monday 9, as he
wanted to give me a mission. I replied, all
right, and at the stated time I was seated
with six other sisters of the place and
Elders, we received our appointments and
went to work. Having lost my notes I
shall have to depend upon my memory as
to dates and other matters. 
	Tues. 10, the Bishop gave a dinner to the
visiting sisters and Elders. Wednesday eve.
9th a company of carriages drove six miles
to Mountain View to a school house that
stood as a lone sentinel on the surrounding
plain. We were about the first to arrive,
at rather a late hour the house was filled
with all grades from grandfather to babe.
The speakers were in the following order:
Brothers Graham and Mann, myself, Sister
Haskell and John W. Taylor. We then
returned to Manassa at 12, midnight. 
	Thursday 12, did some visiting as per ap-
pointment. Then we, including seven car-
riages full, drove 20 miles to East Dale.
Held a meeting in the evening, perfect


[Column 1]
freedom prevailed. The speakers were 
Prof. Brimhall of Provo B. Y. Academy,
then Brother Taylor suggested that the
meeting be turned into a testimony meet-
ing. None responding I then felt to speak,
was blessed with a free flow of speech and
testimony. Then John W. Taylor address-
ed the Saints upon a variety of subjects,
and gave much good counsel pertaining to
the little Colony which was laboring under
difficulties and had succeeded in getting a
fine reservoir, but their surroundings were
yet quite rude with few comforts, while en-
deavoring to do the most important thing
first, viz., to bring under cultivation their
farming lands.
After the meeting had dismissed the com-
pany was still entertained by our quartett
and our Professor of the violin in a very
pleasing manner, a few songs and recita-
tions brought us to a late hour of the night.
The very cool mountain breeze gave ns,
through the chinkings of the house,
refreshing sleep. Winter clothing and bed-
ding seemed to be in demand in the middle
of August.
Friday 13,at 7 o’clock,I witnessed for the
first time the solemn and heavenly cere-
monies of the dedication of the ground for
the erection of a house of worship; with
prayer, singing and speeches, the company
forming a circle of less than one hundred in
number.
We then visited a house that ex-
hibited some nice specimens of pottery. The
lady seemed happy in making presents of
these specimens to some of the visiting
ladies. We then, on our way home, drove
upon the rim of the reservoir which showed
us the immense labor that had been done.
After another twenty miles ride we arrived
at Manassa very tired. After dinner we
again hitched up and drove ten miles to
Antonita, to fill an appointment in a large
new hall that had been freely given for a
meeting. It was filled to overflowing, even 
to the porch, sidewalks and standing on dry
goods boxes in the street. I here listened
to the most interesting and powerful ser-
mon I ever heard, the speakers were Prof.
Brimhall and John W. Taylor. They
divided their subjects and interspersed them
with songs of Zion by the Quartette, and
solos by Prof. White on the violin. The
meeting was a grand success. Sister Has-
kell and I were invited to stay all night but
returned to Manassa, arriving at 12,
midnight.
Saturday 14, drove to Sanford seven
miles, held a council meeting and did some
more visiting. Some of the brethren walk-
ed many miles. I staid all night with the
Bishop’s people, we were treated with every
attention that was in the people’s power
wherever we went.
Sunday 15, attended a conference both
fore and afternoon. The missionaries,
Elders Graham, the two Brothers Mann,
Grant, Ensign, White, Pres. Albert R.
Smith and John W. Taylor addressed the
Saints of several hundred, both fore and
afternoon. The spirit of the Lord was
wonderfully and powerfully poured out up-
on the young men, which must have added
great testimony of the power of God to
some of the weak as well as the strong. To
me it seemed as a “pentacostal” shower.
Their remarks were enriched by the
singing of the Quartette and music by the
Prof, of the violin the music was composed
by some of the Elders. Between meet-
ngs the brethren were required to fast and

[Column2]
hold a Priesthood meeting. In the inter-
im the brethren and sisters were either out
riding or walking, or filling their appoint-
ed visits to families or isolated individuals.
The most of our company returned to 
Manassa.
Monday, August 16, the most of our
group took dinner at Sister Haskell’s. At
1 o’clock several hundred people had ar-
rived by the running stream, and listened
to remarks by the Elders, after which
Apostle John W. Taylor went into the
water and baptized forty-seven souls, a few
of them were re-baptisms and two were for
their health. It seemed to me the most
solemn and impressive services I ever ex-
perienced. I thought of Jesus being bap-
tized in the Jordan by John, which after
was followed by His crucifixion, and of the
approach of His second coming in the near
future; and I questioned who will be pre-
pared for His coming ?
Tuesday 17, we took dinner in company
with a few at Brother Harrison’s. Here
Elder Taylor told me not to be in a hurry,
and he thought I ought to spend the rest of
my life among the people in a spiritual
capacity, and that I had done a good work
there; but here I must stop, as I presume
his encomiums were more interesting to me
than they can possibly be to others. Brother
Taylor is very kind and unassuming with
his colaborers, but when officiating in his
office as a servant of God his words are ac-
companied with great power, and his testi-
mony seems to bring conviction to every
honest heart. Here I bade them good bye
and hurried on six miles to Sanford to fill
an appointment at 3 o’clock. As I arrived
the sisters were already assembled in the
hall, the seats were pretty well filled, they
numbered about ninety. The time occu-
pied by myself was about 12 hours, per-
fect order prevailed, and the spirit of peace,
harmony and rest was such that the many
babes were lulled to sleep, to the admira-
tion of all. A prophecy was made and ful-
filled before we left the house. The spirit
that seemed to prevail prompted a variety
of topics, then after the close of the meeting
the hearers gathered in front for additional
instruction and blessings, after which we
went home to comfort some of the sick in
their houses, after which I returned too
late for the evening meeting and retired
for the night at the Bishop’s.
Wednesday 18, about 4:30 o’clock I left
Sanford in company with Brother Peterson,
wife and child for Hooper, which lies al-
most due north forty-six miles, arriving
five and a half miles northwest of Hooper
station about 8 o’clock, passing on the way
the town of Alamosa. 
Your sister in the Covenant,
DR. ELVIRA S. BARNEY.

MRS. CYNTHIA WESTOVER ALDEN is
now editor of the woman’s department of
the New York Tribune, which is given an
entire page every day with three on Sun-
day, and employs a full staff of reporters,
artists and writers on special subjects, all of
them women.

	MISS SUSAN B. ANTHONY attended the
celebration of the one hundredth birthday
anniversary of the lamented Samuel J. May
by the equal suffragists in Syracuse, N. Y.,
on Oct. 21, and started the same evening
for the meeting of the National Woman’s
Council at Nashville, Tenn.		—Ex.


W. H. P. S. 

	THE Woman’s Hygienic Physiological
Society met in the 14th Ward meeting
house Oct. 7, 1897. There was a good at-
tendance and the workers were very much
encouraged by the presence and remarks of
the brethren. President Angus M. Cannon,
President David Cannon, Bishop Keeler of
Provo, Brother Lucius Snow of Brigham
City, and many others were present. Each
of these brethren expressed themselves as
being pleased with the subjects treated,
they knew it would increase as it was truth.
	Sister Sorensen briefly explained the
work; she said the raising of children was
the most important part of her teaching,
that a woman must understand what is re-
quired of her as a mother, how to care for 
her body that she may bear the healthiest
and noblest of God’s children
	Sister Kirkham of Provo, treated the sub-
ject of “Child Training,” and said we must
be particular in the language we use to a
child and treat them as we wish to be
treated.
	Sister Salzner spoke of the necessity of
girls being prepared for marriage. Sister
Pine described a woman as a nurse in her
own home, what her qualifications should
be etc. Sister Eva Merril of Lehi read a
paper on “Round Dancing.” She said
we ought to obey the authorities of the
Church and not allow impure or immoral
men to be so familiarly associated with us,
such as are often met in the ball room.
Sister Allen prepared and read an essay on
“Prenatal Influence.”
		JULIA S. WOOLLEY, Asst. Sec.
