For the Exponent.
SISTER AND DAUGHTER.

WITHIN the compass of these magic words,
What thoughts will crowd, and stir within the
heart!
Sweeter in tone than instrument or birds,
Although their tones oft bid rich echoes tart.
For there is music in a sister’s name,
And in her acts affection is enshrined;
Beloved at home, is all such ask of fame,
Where every wish and dream is intertwined!
Projects and loves and secrets not a few,
Are i[-]terchanged, yet sacred as is meet;
No friend though o d, no change how fresh or new,
Within the heart finds welcome half so sweet.
In infant years and childhood ever near.
Sisters—yet daughter, claiming mother love,
What ties so tender, what o life so dear,
Or who so looked for in the words above?
For mother, sister in that glorious sphere,
Preserve thou sacred ties which Love doth weld,
And heaven wou d be no heaven, I sadly fear,
If deathless circles ne’er the loved ones held!
Part of our life! we grow together here,
Sisters and daughters, mother—one, yet three,
And through eternal ages, it is surely clear,
Wherever one is, the rest will wish to be!
God has implanted in the depths profound,
That germ divine which links each soul to Him,
And loving makes us worthy to be crowned,
Amid that glory Ages cannot dim!
N.


NEVER DESPAIR.

WORK on e'en though the way be dark and drear,
And heavy clouds around us gathering,
Cause us to doubt success and fear despair,
Have faith, God holds in trust a better day,
And surely, sunshine is beyond the c ouds;
They soon will break and let the sunshine
	through;
A glorious vision then we'll surely see,
When peace and comfort all the world will find;
And rum no more shalf rule God's beauteous land.
Oh! how we'll sing our hallelujah then,
And make the glad world with the good news
ring.
Each one de[-]pairing weakens other's hope,
And makes it harder still to win the cause,
Fight on and never give a good cause up,
Until the battle we can win and shout,
A glorious victory for the cause of God.
I would not have you fight with sword or gun,
That is a barbarous way of olden times;


But God has taught us better, surer ways;
If we obey His orders and commands,
He then will teach us how to win His cause,
	God ne’er forsakes, if man will just believe,
Have faith and trust and work along with Him,
And then in time His cause is sure to win,
If we’ll but keep our shou der to the wheel,
With patience work until God sees the time
Has come, when we sha l victory proclaim.
But never lose our faith and cease to work;
For that would but delay the happy day,
When we can shout and make the whole air ring,
Glo y to God, our righteous cause is won.
MARTHA SHEPARD LIPPINCOTT.
Moo istown, N. J.


[Column 1]
A STRONG TESTIMONY.

Editor Woman's Exponent:
	AFTER reading in the "Woman's Ex-
ponent" of the goodness of our Heavenly
Father manifested towards the women of
Utah, it causes my heart to long for the
time to come when I can be with them and
enjoy the same blessings. The Saints here
in England enjoy many blessings for which
they are very thankful, but they know
there are blessings in Zion that they cannot
have here.
	I belonged to the Church of England for
thirty years, and endeavored in my weak
way to follow out its teachings. I did not
have a strong enough foundation for my
faith, and hence it was too weak to bring
me joy and happiness. I remember how I
used to ponder over in my mind the time of
our beloved Savior and His Apostles, and
wonder to myself why we were not privi-
leged to enjoy the same blessings that they
enjoyed in those days. How strange! Is it
possible the Savior would bestow upon His
children in those days all these promised
blessings and then neglect us who are just
as devoted as they were? These thoughts
often passed through my mind and caused
me to entertain the idea that the church I
had belonged to for so long was not identi-
cal with the Church Jesus established while
here upon the earth.
	In consequence of this I began to feel
unsettled and wondered why such should
be the case. I then began to read and
pray, that I might learn something more of
the true church of Christ, and for over two
years I went from church to church in
search of the one that most resembled the
one established by the Savior. I continued
doing this until I became almost discouraged
but am thankful to say the hand of Provi-
dence was over me, and I was led to a fam-
ily of Latter-day Saints who invited me to
attend their meetings. This led to an in-
vestigation of what they taught, and in a
short time I learned the great reason for all
the different sects of Christianity being void
of the "gifts and blessings" that character-
ized the Primitive church. I learned of
the great apostacy that had swept over the
earth, and how the Lord in consequence of
this had taken from the midst of His people
the everlasting Gospel and all the gifts and
blessings which were enjoyed by those who

[Column 2]
complied with its principles.
	I remember how the remarks of the
Elders would impress themselves upon my
mind when refering to the restoration of
the Gospel and its gifts and blessings.
What faith true men have, I thought. They
promise me every blessing my heart has de-
sired for so many years past if I will go
down into the waters of baptism and re-
ceive a remission of my sins. They then
promise me the Holy Ghost by the laying
on of hands, which shall be my instructor
and comforter through life. These great
and glorious promises deserve my strict at-
tention. This was the first time in my life I
had ever been promised the same blessings
and privileges that were enjoyed by the
Former-day Saints, and I assure you my
heart leaped for joy. I almost feared the
promises too great to be true; but today I
have a testimony which I have received
from God in consequence of my throwing
aside my pride and obeying the sacred prin-
ciples of the Everlasting Gospel. The
power of the Priesthood manifested under
the hands of His authorized servants upon
my little children has been sufficient to con-
vince the whole world, if they would but
lay aside their prejudice and seek the Spirit
of the Lord to guide them into the only
true path of life and salvation.
	I remember of being so low in sickness
on one occasion that the doctor had
given me up to die, but the Elders came in
and laid hands upon me, and before their
hands were taken off my head all swelling
disappeared and the pain left me. Not
many weeks ago my husband met with a
serious accident while at work in the coal
pit, some of his bones being squeezed out of
place. People who saw him and under-
stood what he had passed through ex-
pressed themselves as believing him to be
in such a condition that he would never be
able to work any more. Again the power
of the Lord was manifested. Just as soon
as he was brought home the Elders laid
their hands upon him and anointed him
with oil; in a week he was back at work
again.
	I joined the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints in the year 1880, and dur-
ing that time all the blessings promised me
by the Elders whom I first met have been
granted.
	May the blessings of the Lord shine up-
on the women of Utah, and make them
bright and shining lights is the prayer of
Your sister in the Gospel,
JANE WILLIAMS.
Lancashire, England, Feb. 28, 1898.


EVERY goal in England and Wales now
has a Discharged Prisoners’ Aid Society
working in connection with it.
	THE REAL TROUBLE.—When her Majes-
ty the Queen was at Balmoral some time
ago she visited an aged cottager, and on
leaving said, “You will now, I hope, be no
longer afraid of me, and I shall expect you
to pay me a visit.” “Ah ma’am,” the old
woman replied, “its not yersel’ I’m fricht-
ened at; it’s them gran’ servants!”


WOMAN S EXPONENT


[Column 1]
A SAD OCCURRENCE.

Dear Sister Wells:
	WE are called upon to record a terrible
calamity that has befallen our little colony
in the sudden and dreadful death of our be-
loved sister, Agnes A. McDonald, wife of
Patriarch Alexander F. McDonald, who was
ruthlessly murdered by Mexicans on the
23rd of February last.
	Quite a number of settlers had gone to
Colonia Juarez, a distance of forty miles, to
attend the quarterly conference. She was
the post mistress of our colony and also
kept a small store, and it is supposed this
was what induced the wretches to commit
the horrible deed. She was a great deal of
the time the only occupant of the house,
though her son James lived just across the
street and other neighbors lived near.
	From the appearance of things the
wretches must have been watching her un-
til she retired, when crushing the window
in with an ax they leaped in, and from the
position she was found in it is supposed on
hearing the crash she sprang for the gun
(which she always kept hanging by the
bed) when the villians seizing her, threw
her back upon the bed and cruelly choked
her to death, then placed a  pillow over her
face and proceeded to go through every-
thing in the house and store room, taking
bolts of cloth, ready made shirts, overalls,
jumpers etc., and finding the box in which
she kept the cash, it being locked they took
it with them. In this position she was
found the next morning by her daughter in-
law who called her name repeatedly, and
received no answer. She went a little way
from the house to the cellar where they kept
vegetables, then returned and called again;
receiving no answer she tried the door, it
yielded. On looking in she saw everything
in confusion in the entry. She then passed
into the bedroom where the contents of
trunks, boxes and drawers lay strewed pro-
miscuously over the floor and glass all over
the room. She then summoned up all her
courage and went to the bed, where she
found the poor unfortunate woman with her
feet hanging from the bed and the cover
thrown over her body, and the pillow over
her face. To say that poor Ettie was para-
lyzed for the moment would be poorly ex-
pressing it. She then ran and gave the
alarm.
	The most active of the villians is sup-
posed to be a Mexican who a short time ago
had been working for them, for he knew
well the situation of the house and sur-
roundings, and just where she kept every-
thing. It seems that the deed was commited
in the early part of the night, as they seemed
to have taken their time in ransacking the
house and then rode leisurely away.
	Brother Peas who was hauling lumber
and was camping about three miles north of
here, heard a disturbance among his animals
and looking up to learn the cause saw four
horsemen riding by. It being very dark he
could not tell who they were, but supposed
they were settlers from the Colonies. In
the morning on looking for his harness he
missed a very valuable large horse, but
found the hobbles in the road where they
seemed to have been dropped.
	Morning found the settlers in pursuit of
the murderers and learning of the dreadful
deed Brother Peas very naturally concluded
that they were the thieves and he too joined
in the pursuit. They tracked the rascals by
the tracks made by the large horse, they

[Column 2]
went down the mountain road, passing
lumber teams, horsemen etc., from whom
they gained some information, until they
reached the Casas Grandes river where they
found the horses the rascals had been riding
and packing, including the large horse.
The officers stationed guards at every point
and had every house searched in and around
the city of Casas Grandes. We earnestly
pray that the officers will succeed in captur-
ing the assassins and secure their punish-
ment.
	Sister Agnes was born in Glasgow, Lan-
arkshire, Scotland, Sep. 8, 1838, her par-
ents were Alexander and Janette Aird. She
emigrated to Utah in 1864, crossed the
plains in ox teams and settled in Salt Lake
City. In 1866 she became the wife of
Alexander F. McDonald who is well known
throughout Utah, Arizona and Old Mexico.
She then moved to Provo, thence to St.
George, Utah, thence to Mesa City, Arizona,
and thence to Colonia, Juarez, Mexico,
where she resided until 1895, when she
came to this place where as in former places
she has lived, she has done a great work
and gained the good will of all who knew
her. On January 5, 1896, she was chosen
president of the Relief Society, which posi-
tion [-]he has ably filled, showing her sterl-
ing worth and qualifications to fill the
position.
	Her funeral services were held at 10 a.
m. Friday Feb. 25. The speakers were
Patriarch Henry Lunt, James Jensen,
Bishop George W. Hardy, B. L. Johnson
and L. T. Whetten, who gave good instruc-
tion and words of encouragment. Her re-
mains were then placed on a bier and borne
by eight brothers holding the priesthood,
followed by a procession of carriages, wag-
ons and horsemen.
	The sawmills, shinglemills and all kinds
of labor were suspended and a goodly num-
ber of brethren and sisters came from
Pacheco to attend the services, showing re-
spect and sympathy to our beloved sister
and for her devoted husband and children:
the former being absent on very important
business in El Paso, Texas, was not able to
get here in time for the funeral.
PHEBE I. ALLRED, (Cor.)
	Colonia, Chihuahua, Mexico Feb. 26.


A NEW GRANARY AND HALL. 

Editor Exponent: 

	A SUBSTANTIAL and commodious granary 
has recently been erected by the Beaver Re-
lief Society, which will enable them to con-
tinue the good work of gathering and stor-
ing grain for many years to come without 
being cramped for storage room, (which has 
been the case with them for several years.)
The building is of a beautiful magnesium 
rock and is about fifty feet long by twenty 
wide and a sixteen foot ceiling, and is di-
vided into two rooms. One portion has 
been fitted up as a granary 24 by 25 inside
measurements, and the other portion, which 
is not yet completed, is designed to be em-
bellished with a fine porch, and will make
an excellent apartment for a store or 
other business purposes. 
	The work of the building was projected 
in the early part of the year 1895, at a time 
when money was scarce and the Society 
was practically without any available funds. 
Confidence in the Lord that He would open


up the way, and in the people that they 
would be generous in the assistance they 
were called upon to render to further the 
interests of the work, was felt by the pre-
siding officers; and stimulated by this a 
commencement was made in the summer of 
1895. During that year a solid foundation 
was laid, and during the following year 
the walls were put up, the roof completed 
and a beautiful concrete floor laid in the 
granary, and last year the wheat was trans-
ferred from the old building, the grist mill 
and other places where the Society had 
been under the necessity of storing it, to the 
new granary, approximating one thousand 
bushels, where it is safe from the ravages of 
mice, rats and all other marauders, as the 
building is absolutely vermin proof. 
	In commemoration of this notable event 
in the history of the Beaver Relief Society 
a complimentary picnic and social party was 
extended to all who had in any manner con-
tributed to the enterprise, as also those who 
had been employed in the construction of 
the edifice, on Thursday, Feb. 10, in the 
Assembly hall. 
	The exercises commenced at 11 a. m. by 
singing and prayer, which was followed by 
an extended program of speeches, recita-
tions, songs etc., lasting until 2 p. m. when 
a bountiful supply of edibles was passed 
around to the assembled guests. The re-
freshments consisted of numerous courses 
of delicious sandwiches, pies, cake etc., 
which amply testified to the culinary 
achievements of the cooks. After the feast 
of good things had been disposed of the 
program of exercises was continued until 5 
p. m., when the benediction was pronounc-
ed and the people dispersed with many ex-
pressions of appreciation at the successful 
termination of the efforts of the promoters 
of the social event of the season. 
MARY F. WHITE, Sec.


MADAME MOUNTFORD. 

	ON Monday afternoon the 14th Feb., 
we received a call from Madame Mountford,
the noted Palestine lecturer. She had just 
arrived that morning from New York. 
During the early part of 1897 this talented 
lady spent some three months in Utah.
While there she lectured in all the principal
cities and towns of the State, and became 
quite attached to the people. From Utah
she went on to California, returning to Salt 
Lake in July to attend the pioneer jubilee
celebration. In January of this year on her 
return from the Pacific coast she again 
visited Salt Lake City and on Sunday Janu-
ary 9, spoke at the afternoon services held 
in the Tabernacle, concluding her remarks 
by bidding the people a fond farewell be-
fore returning to her home in Jerusalem. 
She paid a high tribute to the Latter-day 
Saints and their venerable president, Wil-
ford Woodruff. 
	Being so favorably impressed with our 
people and feeling so much at home in their 
midst she has taken the trouble to hunt up 
the missionaries on her way as she journeys 
along. She expects to spend a few months 
in England before continuing her journey. 
	Madame Mountford is a lady of culture 
and refinement. She commands a dignified 
yet easy and natural presence. In conver-
sation she is very entertaining. Her ideas 
are liberal, and she has a good word for all 
nationalities and peoples with whom she 
comes in contact in her extensive travels. 
-Millenial Star.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


MOANINGS.

Be still my heart! ye flutterings cease, 
And calmly wait the dawn of peace !
Rude winds have roughly swept thy strings—
In minor chords it wailing sings.

The sea of life is raging, wild surges roll !
Dark sorrow’s wave engulfs my soul,
But softly borne o’er the water’s foam
Hear the wistful sigh “’Tis not thy home.”

Moaning it falls on the listening ear;
Floats high then low o’er the water drear;
But clearer it sounds when the wind’s wild roar
Blows the music near from the far off shore. 

Arise my soul and spread thy weary wings!
For hope a lu’laby sweetly sings;
Like a light, it beckons o’er the stormy sea,
And whispers so softly “Come to me, come to me.'’

If all days were sunny, the sunsets all fair,
Then never another’s woe would we share;
If heartaches and crosses we never had borne.
Had never our bosoms been pierced with a thorn,

Then never the love that the Master has given,
Would grow in our lives to draw us to heaven;
If never a wanderer far o’er the sea’s foam,
Then never we’d know of the pleasures of home.

“Come to me,come to me”is stil whispered low—
O cling to thy 1 ord though many tears flow;
Still hoping and striving whatever betide,
Till the fair shore is reached where the angels
	abide.
					LYDIA D. ALDER.
Jan.1898.


FAREWELL TO THEE. 

	[Song written in honor of Brother Ezra Steven-
son and sung at his farewell party.]

THOU blest son of Zion we bid thee adieu,
The nations are calling for men brave and true,
To bring them the Gospel and show them the
	way,
Revea’ed by the Father in this Latter-day.

			CHORUS.

	Farewell to thee, farewell to thee,
	We sadly, yet proudly say farewell to thee.

So noble thy mission, thy calling divine,
The powers and blessings of heaven are thine,
To wield for His glory and in His great name,
Perform mighty wonders and Israel redeem.

			CHORUS.

Though oft we shall miss thee we bid thee God
	speed,
And pray that the nation thy message may heed,
May angels go with thee thy footsteps to guide,
Until thou returnest with us to abide.

			CHORUS.

If sometimes thy pathway is lonely and drear, 
May fair visions of home like sunshine appear,
Though sinners pursue thee and scoffers de ide,
Remember we love thee what e’er may betide.

R. M. F.


QUEEN MARGHERITA of Italy has made
herself responsible for the execution of a
plan to build an observatory at the summit
of Monte Rosa, at a height of 4,560 metres.
The work on it has already been begun.
The queen, who, as is well known, is an
enthusiastic mountaineer, is in the habit of
spending several weeks each year at the
summit of Monte Rosa. The observatory
will be higher than that on Mount Blanc,
and the highest in Europe. The cost will
be about $50,000.


Published by request.
AN EVENING WITH “MUSINGS AND
MEMORIES.”

OVATION TO EMMELINE B. WELLS.
	NEVER were the finer instincts of true
poetic natures more richly feasted than on
the evening of February 26, 1898, when an
ovation was given to Sister Emmeline B.
Wells in honor of the publication of her
poetical writings, “Musings and Memories.”
	Over a hundred ladies all friends, “tried
and true,” of the gifted lady, met at the
residence of Dr. Ellis R. Shipp, 75 Center
St. After cordial greetings and congratu-
lations the hostess was given the honor of
chairman. The program was composed al
most entirely from Mrs. Wells’ poems—and
opened with the Ladies singing “Our
Mountain Home so Dear.” Our veteran
Mother in Israel, Sister M. Isabella Horne,
then drew us near to the throne of God
with sacred invocation. The chairman
then called the name of each lady, who re-
sponded with a sentiment from the little
book whose birth we were celebrating.
Miss Olea Shipp sang “True Friendship”
in her sweet contralto, after which Mrs.
Lydia D. Alder gave an elaborate and en-
tertaining review of the book, which was
listened to with enrapt attention. Mrs.
Maggie Bassett and Mrs. Pearl Fernstrom,
nee Russell, sang in their sweetest voices and
Miss Ellis R. Shipp read “The Happy
Maiden.”
	How often have we felt our hearts thrilled
by childrens’ voices singing “Little Chil-
dren Love the Savior,” but never did the
words sound sweeter than at this time when
it was sung by ten little girls in white
dresses, white flowers in their hair and
creamy roses in their hands. The senti-
ment of loving gratitude of thousands of
childish hearts was expressed when they
laid their tribute of flowers in the author’s
lap, receiving in turn kisses and sweet
smiles through dewy tears.
	The name of Dr. R. B. Pratt was then
called who in a few well chosen words pre-
sented to Mrs. Wells in behalf of many
friends, a beautiful book which was a poem
in itself—it being unique in that it was en-
tirely original, each page representing in-
dividual work of nearly a hundred ladies.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells:—Your book of poems,
a long looked for treasure, is now a beauti-
ful, tangible fact and its perusal gives us
sweet pleasure, profit and consolation.
	It is a shining leaf in your “laurel
wreath” of fame with which you stand en-
crowned on your seventieth birthday anni-
versary.
	Your sheaf of years, bound with the
golden cord of love, sympathy and helpful-
ness towards your many friends is fraught
with histories both sweet and sad, but
borne with such fortitude, energy and full
fruition of noble works as is encompassed
rarely in the lives of mortals.
	May your remaining days be less ardu-
ous but even more full of desires accom-
plished; and may the sun of peace and
prosperity and fame ever grow brighter as
you wend your way toward the sunset of
life.
	This book of flowers and sentiments
which I have the honor to present to you,
reflects in every leaf the love and affection
and taste of an individual friend. May it in
days to come be a sweet reminder of our

[Column 2]
days of friendship and association, and buoy
up your heart, if perchance a shadow may
darken an hour.
“Our hearts desire for thee more than we can ex-
	press.
Long life, true joy and an eternal happiness.”
								R. B. P.
	At the close of the literary exercises all
repaired to the dining room to partake of
delicious refreshments amid the cheerful
hum of voices, the perfume of fragrant flow-
ers and sweet strains of music floating
through the rooms, enlivening the hours
and exhilarating the soul.
	True poetry is the divine music of the
inner soul! To feel its instinctive power,
to listen to its silent whisperings, and more
than all, to be able to express its euphoni-
ous eloquence is to be wafted (so to speak)
from our materialistic beings into the realms
of the infinite.
	Those who peruse “Musings and Memor-
ies” must surely feel this mental exaltation.
How many useful lessons we learn from
these truthful touches of nature—“The
Spirit of Consolation,” Sorrow and Sym-
pathy,” “Faith and Fidelity,” the rambles
through woodland and glen and in “The
Dear Old Garden,” the voice from the
mountain and the plaintive, weird music of
the sea—all reach the inmost soul and lure
us back to heaven. The grandest thoughts
of human souls are thus immortalized and
live again within the heart of ages yet un-
born. And methinks in that blest home
where Father dwells, angelic hearts will
sing these songs of praise—these gems of
inspiration—that come to mortals here be-
low as beams of heavenly light from His
exalted presence.
				ELLIS R. SHIPP.


THE CLEOFAN.

	ON Tuesday afternoon the Cleofan enjoy-
ed a topic on the Fairy Queen, by Mrs.
Clara M. Clawson. The Epithalamium
was given by Mrs. Mary J. Clawson. Spen-
cer as a Philosophic Poet, by Mrs. Margie
D. Jennings. All were given in a clear
and concise manner. The club adjourned
for one week.
	March 1, 1898.
	On Tuesday afternoon the Cleofan en-
joyed character readings from Richard III.
by Mrs. Susie D. R. Wells, Mrs. Annie S.
Young, Mrs. Margie D. Jennings and Mrs.
Clara M. Clawson. Mrs. Susie D. R.
Wells gave the story of the play in a most
nteresting manner. The club adjourned
for one week.
	March 8, 1898.
	ON Tuesday afternoon the Cleofan en-
joyed a discussion of Miss M. J. Evans’
paper “The school a moral faction in the
nation,”—by Mrs. Susie D. R. Wells.
Miss. May gave a charming talk on the
Kindergarten system. Reports for the pub-
lic schools were given by Miss Emeline
Wells, Miss Kate Wells, Mrs. Evelyn Y.
Davis and Mrs. Blanche P. Lee. The club
adjourned for one week.
	March 15, 1898.
CLARA T. FELT, Cor. Sec. pro tem.

was defeated Feb. 15, in the Upper House
of the Iowa Assembly, 50 to 47.

SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 15 & APRIL 1, 1898.


RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE.

	THE general conference of the Relief So-
ciety will be held on Tuesday, April 5,
1898, in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall in
this city, meetings commencing at 10 a. m.
and 2 p. m. as usual. It is very desirable
that there should be representation from
each Stake organization of the Society, eith-
er the president or one of her counselors
should attend or authorize some one to act
for the board, All members of the Society
are cordially invited, also the Y. L. M. I. A.
and Primary. Presidents, Bishops and
brethren will be most welcome.
	There will be an officers’ meeting on
Saturday, April 9, at the same place, com-
mencing at 10 a. m. and all officers of the
Society are urgently requested to attend.
ZINA D. H. YOUNG
President.
JANE S. RICHARDS
BATHSHEBA W. SMITH,
Counselors.


THE WAR CRY.

	FOR some time past a war cry has been
in the air all over this country and in
other lands and to many war has seemed in-
evitable. The telegraphic news has been
conflicting yet on the whole it has sound-
ed threatening, to put the matter mildly.
The explosion of the Maine has formed
the topic for weeks, and now that the
Court of Inquiry has rendered its verdict,
there seems little change in the general
commotion. Preparations for war in the
army and navy are going forward and the
outlook is not a happy one to say the
least.
	At last President McKinley has spoken;
his message on the subject including the
findings of the court, were presented to the
Congress of the United States Monday,
March 28, and was received with intense
interest. Congress took deliberate and dig-
nified action—the message was referred to
Committees.
	The times are war-like in aspect and
many “wise men of Gotham” anticipate
war in the near future. The City of
Havana is reported to be a veritable vol-
cano. War ships and torpedo boats seem
to be engaging the attention of men, and
if one listens to the war talk in public
places, one might easily assume that peace
was indeed taken from the earth.
	However, one fact is apparent, there is
much suffering and destitution. America


is acting nobly in relieving the distressed
and the Red Cross Society are on hand to
take charge of supplies and manage these
affairs economically.
	Spain would show wisdom if she would
consent to the measures proposed by the
American government, for surely she will
be made to yield ignominisously if war is
entered upon. Those who desire an ami-
cable settlement of affairs continue hopeful
and perhaps the cry of war may pass by
this time without involving nations as
now seems not unlikely; but eventually
the prophecies concerning these things
must be fulfilled however much people
who love peace may regret the necessity of
war.


[Column 1]
F. MARION CRAWFORD.

	THE distinguished author and lecturer,
F. Marion Crawford, recently paid Utah a
visit speaking in Provo, Salt Lake and
Ogden.
	Mr. Crawford is an American though
born in Rome, where his father (Thomas
Crawford) was at the time studying sculp-
ture. He has had a cosmopolitan educa-
tion—has studied at Harvard and at Cam-
bridge, England, and Heidleburg, Germany,
has traveled very extensively and at one time
edited a newspaper in Allahabad, India; he
has lived much in foreign parts, principally
in Italy and Sicily. He is a man of broad
views and has a decided originality that is
very charming. He is very tall and of mag-
netic presence, has a rich, clear voice and
eloquent eyes, his diction is perfect, one
never loses a word, nor even an accent.
With most of his books the people of Salt
Lake City are familiar and unlike most
authors he was evidently willing to talk of
his stories.
	His lecture in this city was under the
auspices of the Ladies Literary club and
was given in the M. E. Church on Third
South St. Mr. Crawford was introduced to
the audience by Rev. Henry, who had
known him in New York City. The audi-
ence was large, the hall being crowded to
its utmost capacity. The subject was Leo
XIII. and the Vatican. Evidently the
speaker was in love with his theme; it was
apparent throughout the evening, for he
spoke nearly two hours. He described the
Pope graphically, both the outward and the
inner man; one who heard him could easily
see the picture and imagine the voice and
manner of the man he portrayed. A scholar,
a poet, a statesman, brilliant in intellectual
attainments, cultured and polished in all
that gives tone and finish to the gentleman
and scholar, and yet simple in tastes and
habits in the details of life. Mr. Crawford
compared Pope Leo with Lincoln and
Gladstone and contrasted him and his
labors in the Church with Pope Pius IX.
	The Utah Woman’s Press Club gave a
reception to Mr. Crawford at the Bee Hive
House—the residence of Hon. Spencer
Clawson—from 3 to 6 p. m. on Tuesday,
March 22. The parlors were prettily ar-
ranged for the occasion and Miss Claire
Clawson in her sweet, amiable way made
the ladies of the club more than welcome.
Dr. R. B. Pratt, E. B. Wells and Susa Y.
Gates took charge of the introductions and
presented the callers to Mr. Crawford, the
guest of honor, who was most gracious and
affable, conversing with ease and freedom
to each one present with a dignified con-
geniality that won praise and admira-

[Column 2]
tion from all hearts. A little good
music, Prof. Cornelius Schettler on the gui-
tar, and some fine selections on the piano.
Miss Carol Cannon sang in her best and
sweetest style, “Because,” and Lulu Gates
in her childlike way with rich pathos ren-
dered three choice songs, a Florentine song
by Benjamin Goddard “The Dream” and
“The Garden of Sleep.” The guests were
about a hundred and fifty in number and
included Col. Kent and wife, Lieut. Gurney,
Mrs. Cabaniss, Mrs. Tayman of the Fort
and Mrs. Patterson of Cleveland, a guest at
the Fort, President Angus M. Cannon,
Elder C. W. Penrose, Dr. M. Hughes Can-
non State Senator, Mrs. W. F. Nelden,
Mrs. John McVicker, Mrs. Robert Walker,
Mrs. W. C. Jennings, Mrs. Arthur Brown,
Mrs. John Q. Cannon, Mrs. Cornelius
Schettler, Mrs. Philo T. Farnsworth and
Miss Julia Farnsworth, Mrs. A. W. Mc-
Cune and Miss Fay McCune, Prof. Mar-
shall and wife, Mrs. Evelyn Y. Davis,
Miss Emeline and Miss Lyde Wells, Mrs.
A. E. Hyde and Miss Laura Hyde, Mrs.
R. K. Thomas, Mrs. M. Salmon of Omaha,
Mrs. K. Verran of Michigan Mrs. S. Clay-
ton of this city, and many others.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	ON Saturday afternoon a meeting was
held in Spring City, Sister Mary Frantzen
presiding. Bishop Allred was present and
made some remarks after the sisters had all
spoken, expressing great interest in the Re-
lief Society and the Latter-day work as a
whole.
	MADAME MOUNTFORD who visited Utah
during the last year and gave her original
entertainments in several places and spoke
in the large Tabernacle in this city on the
Sunday before her departure for the East,
after visiting Toronto, Canada, New York
City and London, sailed from Marseilles on
the steamer “Midnight Sun,” going via
Sicily, Athens, Constantinople, the Holy
Land, Egypt and Malta. No word has
been received of her plans while absent that
we are aware of; she is registered as an
eminent lecturer along with several divines
and professors.
	PRESIDENT ZINA D. H. YOUNG and
Sister Emily S. Richards of this city went
to Ogden and attended the Relief Society
conference of Weber Stake held on Thurs-
day, March 10, in that city, Sister Jane S.
Richards presiding. There was a goodly
attendance from various parts of the county
and an excellent spirit manifest. The
work in that Stake seems to be rapidly pro-
gressing and the deepest interest is appar-
ent in all the gatherings.
	THE Relief Society conference of Sanpete
Stake was held in Ephraim on Friday,
March 11th and on Saturday morning,
March 12, President Mary A. Hyde presid-
ing. Visitors from this city were Sisters
E. B. Wells and Annie T. Hyde. President
Canute Peterson attended the three sessions
of the conference and President J. T. D.
McAllister was present at the morning ses-
sion on Friday. There were sisters from
most of the settlements in the country, not-
withstanding the cold, stormy weather.
At Ephraim we attended a meeting of the
Primary Association and were much grati-
fied with the interesting program and beau-
tiful children assembled. The young girls 
in charge seemed to love their work and to


[Column 1]
have been successful in gaining the love of
the children.
	A VERY pleasant social was given in the
14th ward Assembly hall in honor of
Brother Ezra Stevenson on the evening of
the 24th of March, previous to his depart-
ure for New Zealand, this being his second
mission to that far off land. Appreciating
his faithfulness in the performance of his
duties, the members of the ward and his
many friends united in the effort to show
their great esteem for him. Hirini Whanuga, 
the Maori chief and family lent their pre-
sence for the occasion. The room was
tastefully decorated with ferns and banners,
the one at the head of the hall bearing the
inscription, “Long live our Superinten-
dent,” another at the side reading, “Your
many friends wish you God speed on your
journey.” An excellent program was
rendered, after which refreshments were
served by the young ladies. All felt to say
“God bless Brother Ezra Stevenson.”
	UTAH has been favored of late with visits
from Governors of other states; the Govern-
or of Utah of whom the people are justly
proud, has had an opportunity to extend
hospitality and welcome to these distinguish-
ed visitors,and maintain the hospitable char-
acter Utah has always been credited with in
the past. Governor Adams and wife of 
Colorado and Governor Richards and wife
of Wyoming were in Salt Lake City at the
same time’ and it was fully expected that
Governor Stonenberg of Idaho would join
the party here, but he did not come. A
dinner at the Alta Club for the Governors
their friends and colleagues, and a lunch-
eon at Col. Clayton’s for the ladies of the
party and other local ladies was a part of
the program of entertainment, a trip to 
Saltair and a reception at the Knutsford
were also features of their sojourn.
	MORE recently still Governor Hastings of
the great state of Pennsylvania, accompan-
ed by his wife and daughter and District
Attorney McCormick wife and daughter,al-
so Senator Cochran wife and daughter, who
had been touring in the West, paid Salt 
Lake City a brief visit and were shown the
principal interesting places etc. The
Governor and his staff escorted the party to
the Tabernacle, the President’s office where
they met Presidents Woodruff and Can-
non of the L. D. S. Church, and to Fort
Douglas where they were welcomed with
military honors and music, also a trip to
Saltair, Col. Kent and party joining with the
Governor and his staff and Attorney General
Bishop with invited guests from the city,
making quite a pleasant party. Col. Kent
kindly furnished musicians from the Fort
which added much to the enjoyment of the
party while at the pavilion, and made the
trip still more interesting.
	ON Sunday evening and Monday
morning meetings were held in Fairview
though the weather was most unfavorable,
yet the people came in large numbers.
At Fairview we were delighted to meet once 
more Sister Mary A. Pitchforth who has 
presided over the Relief Society there from
its first organization. She is very greatly
beloved; at present she is very feeble and
looks frail, but her courage is sublime and
her faith in the work is firm and steadfast.
At Fairview we met with two young men
from the city who were traveling on busi-
ness Elders Melvin D. Wells and Brigham
T. Cannon, the latter just home recently

[Column 2]
from a mission to Germany. They attended
the Relief Society meeting on Sunday eve-
ning and bore a good testimony to the
truth of the Gospel. During the trip all
around President M. A. Hyde, who is near-
ly eighty-two years of age, kept up wonder-
fully and seemed equal to the responsibili-
ties resting upon her in her office and call-
ing; in fact she seemed to improve as we
traveled from place to place.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


CALIFORNIA MISSION.

Dear Editor:
	THE 10th of February being the anniver-
sary of the marriage of President and Mrs.
Nye, the Relief Society tendered them a
dinner at the Mission house. Eighty peo-
ple were seated at the tables which were
laden with all the good things one could de-
sire. The guests were waited on by Miss
Lilly Boedefeld, Miss Flossie Sarll, Miss
Adle Boedefeld Miss Edith Sarll and Miss
Florence Rawlinson, young ladies of the
branch. They were kept busy nearly three
hours and in a most gracious manner waited
upon all who came.
	Many investigators hearing of the event
came to do honor to Brother and Sister Nye
who are ever ready to help in the cause of
truth, one would have thought he was in
Utah to look down the long banqueting
table and see so many well known Utah
people, among whom were Brother Lorin
Farr of Ogden, Don C. Musser, Sister Mary
J. Young and son Briant Young; also Willie
Mackintosh of Salt Lake and many other
former residents of Utah, and ten Elders
laboring in San Francisco and vicinity help-
ed to swell the number
	Sister M. A. Rattenberry, president of
the Relief Society, made a speech while the
guests were still seated at the table, in her
well known and pleasing style. The other
officers, Sisters Sewell, Simpkin, Sarll and
Woodward, followed in neat little speeches
full of loving words to Sister Nye, who
labors with them so faithfully to help in the
mission work in California. The evening
was spent in vocal music and recitations
by members of the branch, and a most en-
joyable time was spent.
	The Relief Society is progressing and the
sisters feel that God is blessing their work.
IDA M. SARLL, Sec.
San Francisco, March 17, 1898.


U.W. P. C.

THE ladies of the U. W. P. C. met as
per adjournment at the “Woman’s Ex-
ponent” office Monday, March 6, 1898.
The president. Dr. R. B. Pratt, being ab-
sent the vice-president, Mrs. R. H. Doolan,
occupied the chair. Prayer was offered by
Mrs. Mary A. Freeze. The roll call was re-
sponded to by quotations from Madame De
Stael. Dr. Orielle Curtis was appointed by
unanimous vote critic for the year.
	Under unfinished business the by-laws of
the club were read and discussion thereon
followed. Mrs. E. B. Wells moved that
Miss Laura Hyde be alternate director in
the State Federation of Utah Woman’s
Clubs, Mrs. L. S. Dickinson being director.
Motion carried.
	The question of issuing receipts for mem-
bership was also presented by Mrs. E. B.
Wells and left over as unfinished business.
She then drew the club’s attention to the
George Washington Association, stating


that Margaret A. Caine had advanced in be-
half of the U. W. P. C. five dollars for
identification as a charter member. A dis-
cussion on ways and means followed and
the question was left as unfinished business.
	The secretary read a letter from the cor-
responding secretary of the Woman’s Club
inviting the appointment or a representative
to meet with them in general council for
discussing important questions of general
interest. Dr. Orielle Curtis nominated
Ruth M. Fox and the motion was carried.
	A letter was read from the acting chair-
man of the U. W. P. C. soliciting donation
of one dollar towards establishing traveling
libraries Motion agreeable to invitation
was carried.
	Mrs. Lawler was admitted as a member
and began the evening’s program with a
charming paper on France. Mrs. Rachel
Miller read one equally as interesting on
Noted Men of France. Mrs. Lizzie Wilcox
gave a delightful short talk on Noted Wo-
men of France. The program was announc-
ed for next meeting, the minutes were read
and with one correction accepted, and the
club adjourned for one month.
Program for next, meeting: Paper on
Spain, Phebe C. Young; Cuba, Dr.
Orielle Curtis; Portugal, Emily S.
Richards. Poem, Dr. Ellis R. Shipp.
Quotations from Spanlsh authors.

M. A. J. LAMBERT, Sec.


TO SISTER L. M. HART.

THE souvenirs presented you
	This day, cannot express
Our loving appreciation
	Of your works of righteousness.
We’ve labored with you many years,
	And proved you ever true,
When you engaged to do a work,
	That you would surely do.

How many times we’ve listened
	To counsels you have given,
To Zion’s daughters to be pure,
	And make on earth a heaven. 
You have taught us age to honor,
	To our dear mothers kind,
That on the earth no other,
	Like mother could we find.

’Tis not alone by precept,
	You’ve pointed out the way,
But by your own example,
	Consistent day by day.
Refinement and intelligence,
	We know that you possess,
And that you seek for wisdom,
	In truth and holliness.

Our martyred Prophet taught the Saints
	That they must knowledge gain,
For they could not in ignorance,
	Eternal life obtain.
And you’re among the faithful,
	Who prize the talents given,
And thus prepare by faith and prayer.
	To dwell with Saints in heaven.

Oftimes have we been welcomed
	Into your pleasant home,
Where rays of lively sunshine.
	Upon our hearts would come.
You’ve been kind unto the orphan,
	You’ve clothed and fed the poor,
And for your acts of kindness,
	God bless you, evermore.


You’ve passed through many trials,
	As all God’s people do;
But He has given strength and power,
	To bravely battle through.
May you have strength sufficient,
	To help you to the end,
Is the prayer most humbly offered,
	For you,our worthy friend.
NANCY ALLRED.
Bloomington, Idaho.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


REEIEF SOCIETY ENTERTAINMENT

	ON Feb. 22 an entertainment was given
in the 8th ward under the auspices of the
Relief Society in honor of the president,
Catharine K. Palmer, to which all the adult
members of the ward were invited. There
was an excellent program under the direc-
tion of J. M. Chamberlain. The Bishop
and his counselors were present, also sever-
al who were once members of the ward.
Refreshments were served and a very en-
joyable time was spent.
			ABBY A. PIGGOTT, Sec.


[Column 1]
LADIES SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of the Ladies Semi-monthly
meeting held in the 14th ward Assembly
hall Saturday Feb. 19, 1898, President M.
I. Horne presiding. Singing “O say what
is truth.” Prayer by Counselor E. Webb.
Singing “How will the Saints rejoice to
tell.” Minutes of the previous meeting
read and accepted.
	President Horne quoted the saying of the
Savior that “If ye are not one ye are not
mine.” She thought the Saints should all
be united in everything they attempt to do
and said we all had a portion of the Spirit
of God, that we brought it with us when
we left the Lord’s presence: it was this that
prompted us to do any good thing and it
remained with us whether we used it to do
good or not. Said it took bravery to be a
Latter-day Saint for we had much to over-
come, but when we kept His command-
ments no good thing either in heaven or on
earth would be witheld from us. She felt
we were indeed a blessed people. In a little
company recently where President Woodruff
was present she heard him say that we
should strive to do the works of Latter-day
Saints, that we would not fall by the way-
side, Urged those present to do those
works and talked very plainly to the Saints.
	Sister Annie O. Brown said she felt it
was necessary to do the works of God, and
knew we should feel like brothers and sis-
ters to one another. The Gospel was every-
thing to her, bore a strong testimony and
felt that this was all she lived for.
	Sister Lydia D. Alder referred to the ter-
rible things happening in the world of
which we read, also of the signs of the times.
She felt we could not help asking ourselves
the question “Will we be able to stand the
test?” We could only trust in God; when
we were in trouble it was then He was the
nearest to us. Spoke of the condition of the
world today, said that people scarcely had
any faith in one another and very little in
God; they feared greatly something but did
not know what; knew this was one of the
signs of the times.
	Sister Carlhurst bore her testimony to
the truth of tithing, and told how by pay-
ing her tithing she was greatly blest.
	Sister Margaret P. Young said she en-
joyed these meetings and the sweet spirit

[Column 2]
that always attended the meetings of the
Saints She realized that God had been
good to her and had ever had a watch-care
over her. She had never felt like drawing
away from Him. Spoke of the life and
works of Elder Erastus Snow. He lived at
her father’s home for some time, had en-
joyed the Gospel then very much as also
now. Said she did not care to be the one
to say she had never had a trial.
	Sister Cottrell said she thought we should
not speak of trials when we were permitted
to come here and attend these meetings and
enjoy the sweet spirit which was here.
She had been reading about some of the
ancient martyrs who had been so cruelly
persecuted for their religion, and could not
help thinking what a blessed people we
were that we could enjoy our religion to
the extent we did; bore a strong testimony.
	Sister Barrow said there was qot a day
when she did not thank God for the bless-
ings she enjoyed and wondered how any
one could doubt, especially when we could
see the prophecies of the prophets of God
being so rapidly fulfilled. Prayed the Lord
to bless our aged sisters, that they might be
spared to us for many years yet to instruct
and encourage us.
	Sister Rawlings said she had been very
ill but was healed by the power of faith, for
which she was very grateful. A sister in
the congregation also bore her testimony to
the power of healing.
	Sister Barnes felt thankful for our dear
aged sisters, Sister Horne and others, who
had been such a blessing and comfort to us.
Spoke of her native country Scotland, said
she loved it, yet she was glad to leave it to
come to Zion. Singing “The Spirit of God
like a fire is burning.”
	Sister Annie T. Hyde felt we had had a
treat in listening to the beautiful testi-
monies of the sisters, and knew we were
strengthened by this. She thought with
Sister Horne that the Spirit of the Lord was
being poured out upon the sisters, and al-
luded to the signs of the times, felt there
was a very eventful time before us. The
mothers should be careful what they said
before their children, that their faith
might not be weakened but strengthened.
Prayed that we might not make much of
our trials but put our trust in God.
	Dr. E. S. Barney said she thought we
should learn to sift ourselves and throw out
the chaff. Spoke on humility and the
signs of the times. Said we must not for-
get our God or He would forget us. When
we get discouraged just throw off the humor
and rest and then with the help of the Lord
we would go on again refreshed.
	Sister Mary Silver said she felt glad that
she was a Latter-day Saint, and she was also
glad and proud that we had a sister like
Sister Horne to help and advise us. Said
we never knew her to tell us anything that
was not true, and spoke on the word of
wisdom, knew there was peace for those
who practiced it.
	Sister Hannah Wright thought it was
good to devote a little of our time to meet-
ings, said the more time we gave to the
Lord the better it was for us, there was no
enjoyment like serving the Lord. She had
heard Brother Woodruff say, “How very
few of the millions that are in the world
have embraced the Gospel and how few of
the Saints are really living their religion.”
She felt pleased and grateful to see so many
here. Said sometimes she wished the Gos-
pel had come to her earlier in life, but was

[Column 3]
consoled by having a brother say to her
that it came in just the right time.
	Singing “Be it my only wisdom here.”
President Horne had been reminded to-
day of a saying of Sister Eliza R. Snow that
“a good meeting was like a picnic where
each one brought something different that
was good.” It was so with the testimonies
this afternoon. Said when this building
would not hold all who came, we would
gladly adjourn to the Assembly hall.
	Adjourned for two weeks. Singing
“Satan’s empire long has flourished.”
Benediction by Dr. E. S. Barney.

	Minutes of the Ladies’ Semi-monthly
meeting held in the 14th ward Assembly
hall Saturday, March 5, 1898, President
M. I. Horne presiding. Singing “Jesus the
Giver.” Prayer by Sister Maria W. Wil-
cox. Singing “Where the voice of friend-
ship’s heard.” Minutes of the previous
meeting were read and accepted.
	President Horne requested the sisters to
be more united in their votes, she would like
to see every hand raised when a vote was
taken. Spoke strongly on fault finding,
especially with our brethren and the author-
ities, said the Lord was not pleased with us
when this was the case and urged the sis-
ters to unite their forces to quell this. Said
that there was nothing would give us the
joy that knowing we had served the Lord
would, and that there would be a place in
our Father’s kingdom for us. Fault find-
ing was not serving the Lord, neither does
it bring us peace or pleasure; it was the
humble and the meek who had the Spirit
of the Lord. She regretted that some of
our young people were married outside the
Temple. Knew this was a day of choosing
as well as warning, spoke strongly on the
signs of the times.
	Sister Holling knew by the minutes that
we must have had a beautiful meeting last
time, and felt the older she grew the strong-
er was her desire to serve the Lord.
	Dr. E. S. Barney said she had been fifty-
four years in learning obedience, that if we
lived right we were entitled to the prompt-
ings of the Holy Spirit. Asked how many
had had their Patriarchial blessings and
urged those who did not have them to get
them; said that they would be a source of
comfort. Spoke on the signs of the times,
also on young people being married right.
	A sister from Cache Valley said she felt
impressed to bear her testimony. She was
glad to see Sister Zina D. H. Young here,
and felt to say God bless her. Said many
times had she put her hands upon her head
and blessed her, also had heard her speak in
tongues many times.
	Singing “You are called to labor.” Sis-
ter Margaret P. Young said she never re-
membered of doubting the truth of the Gos-
pel, she had always been a teacher in the
Relief Society and enjoyed her labors.
Sister Mary A. Hardy felt thankful for the
good women whom we had in our midst,
	Sisters Young and Horne, to comfort and
advise us. We knew the Lord was not
pleased with us when we indulged in fault-
finding and evil speaking against the Lord’s
anointed.
	President Zina D. H. Young said that
while looking around on the faces of her
sisters she was reminded of the Savior’s
words, that those who feared the Lord met
often together and spoke one to another,
“And they will be mine when I come to
make up my jewels.” “Just think of it


my sisters, by coming here and bearing our
testimonies and speaking one to another we
can be His. It is a simple thing to do but
means much.” Said she felt sorry for any
woman who spoke against the revelations
of God. Singing “Our God we raise to
Thee.”
	Sister Elizabeth Eangton said she knew
the Spirit of the Lord was in this meeting
in great abundance, she had been greatly
blessed by the Lord, had been enabled to
send money to her husband who was on a
mission and also to gather a great amount
of genealogy. Said she had been forty
years in the Church, and felt greatly blest
in being able to say this.
	Sister Emma Nunn said she and her little
girl had both been very sick but had been
healed by the power of God. Had prom-
ised the Lord she would speak of His good-
ness to her this afternoon.
	Sister McMaster bore her testimony, felt
if she went away without doing this she
would be condemned. Spoke of her Relief
Society work and how she enjoyed it. She
knew it was the work of God.
	Sister Sarah Phelps made some beautiful
remarks, also spoke in tongues.
	Sister Derrick spoke of her recent be-
reavement in the death of her husband, and
alluded to his many virtues. Told how
suddenly he was taken, that he was almost
changed in the twinkling of an eye bore a
strong testimony. 
	A sister in the congregation said she was
thankful that we had a place like
this where we could meet together and sing
and pray and comfort one another.
	President Horne felt the meeting had
been very enjoyable. Gave out the notice
of Stake conference two weeks from today.
	Adjourned for four weeks. Singing
“This God is the God we adore.” Bene-
diction by Counselor E. Webb.
				ZINA H. BULL, Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

SISTER AGNES M’ARTHUR WHITE.

	Sister McArthur White, daughter of James and
Elizabeth McA thur, was born May 13, 1842, was
baptized into the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints November 1857 and emigrated to Utah
in 1859. She was married to Stephen White in
Salt Lake City on November 1, 1859, and lived in
Mill Creek ward until the spring of 1868 w en she
moved to Willard where she resided until her
death on Feb uary 4, 1898.
	Sister White was a teacher in the Relief Society
of Willard and a counselor in the North District
Primary Association, also a teacher in the
Sunday Schoo . She was a faithfu  Latter-day
Saint and was loved and respected by all who
knew her. She eaves a kind husband and eight
children, four sons and four daughters to mourn
her loss.
		RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT. 
	WHEREAS, Our Heavenl  Fa her has seen fit to
take from our midst one of our sisters, be it
	RESOLVED, That we the officers and members of
the Relief Society of Willard and of the Primary
Association of North District do extend our heart-
felt sympathy to the bereaved family in this their
hour of sorrow, and that we ever cherish her
memory. 
	RESOLVED, Tha  a copy of these resolutions be
placed on the records of the Relief Society, one
presented to the bereaved family and one sent to
the “Woman’s Exponent” for publication.
				BETHIAH F. WELLS, Sec.

	HANNAH CHAPMAN RAYMOND
	Died at Soda Springs, Idaho, Feb. 15, 1898, of
paralysis, Hannah Chapman Raymond. Deceased
was born at Crowle, England, March 2, 1813, and
would have been eighty-five years old had she
lived two weeks longer She joined the Church
of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in England
and emigrated to Utah in 1856, coming across the


plains in the first train of handcarts and belonging
to Brother Ellsworth’s company; she was known
at that time as Sister Goodworth, and traveled a
widow with four children, only one of whom sur-
vives her, viz.,Mrs. Ann Ashby, wife of Benj. Ash-
by of Woods Cross, Utah, who was an invalid at
that time   Deceased was left a widow four times
during her life and has suffered great oss in her
family, being the mother of thirteen children of
whom four only are liv ng, viz. Mrs. Frances
Clark, Crowle, England; Mrs. Ann Ashby, Wood’s
Cross, Utah; Mr William Chester and Mrs. Alice
Cambell, Soda Springs, Idaho. Deceased has
enjoyed particularly good health for ten years un-
til her last sickness, and has kept her own house
d ing most of her own work, until her eyesight
began to fail and some of her grandchildren lived
with her until about two years ago, when she re-
moved to the house of her daughter, Mrs Cam-
bell, where she died.
	She experienced a circumstance which was, no
doubt, miraculous, in connection with the dedica-
tion of of the Temple at Salt Lake. It was a
cherished wish of her’s that she might live to see
the completion of that building, and as the time
drew near her eyesight began to get so poor that
she was unable to see with the aid of glasses.
She attended the services, however, and when
she arrived at the Temple er eyesight was so far
restored that she dis inctly saw all objec s around
her, and after she returned to her daughter’s
home at Wood’s Cross she was able to read a
newspaper and thread a needle without the aid of
glasses. This blessing lasted only for a short
time, but her wish that she might see the Temple
was gratified
	She was president of the Relief Society of Soda
Springs ward for fifteen years, when she was
hono ably released on account of her old a e in
June 1896. She leaves many grandchildren,
great grandchildren, and one little great great
grandchild, the daughter of Cyrus and Emma
Page, of Bountiful Utah, besides a large number
of friends both in and out of the Church to
mourn her loss, for she was a friend to all.

R. S. AND P. A. REPORTS.

ST. JOHN’S STAKE.
	Minutes of the St. Johns Stake Primary con-
ference held Feb. 28, 1898, President Emma
B. Coleman presiding. After singing pray-
er was offered by our young brother, Oscar
Freeman. Sister Coleman made same good
and appropriate opening remarks. after
which a short program was rendered con-
sisting of songs, music, recitations and our
paper read by the editor, Ethel Farr.
	Short reports were given by presidents or
representatives of the different wards, every
ward in the Stake being represented. The
statistical report was read and the officers
sustained.
	Sister Cornelia Horne Clayton of Provo,
Utah, then addressed the congregation
in a very interesting manner, giving good
and timely instruction and sang the chil-
dren a song.
	President D. K. Udall spoke for a short
time, followed by Celia Hamblin. After
singing, benediction was pronounced by
young Revel Jarvis.
	SARAH H. CROSBY, Sec. pro tem.
MARICOPA STAKE.
	Minutes of Relief Society conference held
at the Society hall Dec. 16, 1897, President
Mabel Ann Hakes presiding assisted by her
counselors, Lizzie A. McDonald and Sarah
M. Johnson, President C. R. Hakes and
other sisters Meeting was opened by the
usual exercises. Statistical and financial
report for the year ending Dec. 16, 1897,
was read and accepted. All the wards were
represented showing a good spirit prevail-
ing in our midst and the Saints generally
alive to their duties. The topics dwelt up-
on were the fostering of home industries,
storing grain etc., and a more strict observ-

[Column 2]
ance of the laws of God. A number of the
sisters bore testimonies, and all felt to re-
joice in the great work of the last days.
	President C. R. Hakes exhorted the
Saints to awake and make the warfare
against a spirit of evil and iniquity stronger
and harder until we should have helped to
redeem the inhabitants of the earth.
	Many good seeds were sown in the hearts
of those present, and all felt blest in coming
ttogether in this meeting. Adjourned fo
hree months. Benediction by President
Hakes.
			F. E. ROBSON, Sec.
PAROWAN STAKE.
	ON February 11, 1898, the sisters of the
several branches comprising the Parowan
Stake Relief Society held their seventieth
quarterly conference in the ward Tabernacle
of Cedar City. The weather being un-
favorable the attendance was not so large as
it otherwise might have been. The five
wards comprising the Stake organizations
were represented as progressing favorably:
The work of gathering grain for the time of
need is being systematically carried on by
the sisters, the Stake having 1,480 bushels
and 30 pounds on hand at the present time.
	The instructions during our meeting were
principally exhorting parents, mothers es-
pecially, to train their children in their
youth in the principles of the work we as
Latter-day Saints are engaged in, for the
impressions made in childhood are never
forgotten. We should impress upon our
little ones the necessity of prayer and teach,
them to have faith in the Lord. The sub-
jects of intemperance and the word of wis-
dom were also spoken upon.
	As is customary at our conferences, Stake
President U. T. Jones with his counselors,
Francis Webster and Henry Leigh, and
several other brethren were in attendance
and by request a number of them addressed
the congregation, their good counsel being
duly appreciated by all present.
	The business attended to during the con-
ference was the reading of reports and sus-
taining of local officers. Sister Ann E.
Webster was sustained as first counselor to
Stake President Mary Ann Lunt, thereby
filling the vacancy caused by the death of
Sister Sage T. Jones. Sister Webster acted
in the capacity of president of the Cedar Re-
lief Society from 1886 to 1897, when she
was honorably released, her successor
being Ann A. Thorley with Mary A. Arm-
strong and Lucy M. Jones first and second
counselors.
	During the past year quite a number of
the sisters belonging to the Cedar Society
have been called away by the hand of death
and their presence and their words of
counsel are alike missed. In January two
members, Margaret Unthank and Margaret
Froyd, both faithful good workers in our
Society every ready to assist and for a
number of years acting as teachers, depart-
ed from us, also Sister Amanda Rollo,
another good, faithful member soon follow-
ed. Death is inevitable, hence the necessity
of living not for ourselves alone but by do-
ing good whenever we have the opportuni-
ty, if only by a kind word.
	Conference was adjourned to meet at
Paragoonah in three months.
	J. P. MACKELPRANG, Cor. Sec.
NOTTINGHAM, ENGLAND.
	At the request of our president, Sister
Sarah Barber, and also the members of this


[Column 1]
the Nottingham branch Relief Society, I
pen these few lines to you thinking perhaps
you would like to know how the Lord’s
vineyard is getting along here.
	Sister Barber desired me to tell you of a
social gathering we had at our meeting in
this the new year, which was held at the
home of our president. We had a very en-
joyable time all the evening. All the
members of the Relief Society were present,
also a few non-members. After supper
some of the sisters rendered some good
songs and speeches. A good spirit pre-
vailed throughout and we all felt a renewed
determination to help each other as well as
the poor, and to do all we could to promul-
gate the Gospel.
	It would perhaps interest you to know
that a great many of the members in the
summer go out with the Elders and help
them in the singing, and their labors are
bearing fruit already. We have quite a
few investigators in the branch, some who are
going to be baptized at the first opportunity.
We have very good meetings on Sundays
and a goodly portion of the Holy Spirit is
always present.
	I will close praying that the Spirit of the
Lord will always be with us to guide us in-
to the path of purity and right.
				Your sister in the Gospel
				EDITH CABLE, Sec. R. S.
	February 19, 1898.
RELIEF SOCIETY REORGANIZED. 
	Minutes of 21st ward Relief Society held
in the Society hall Feb. 3, 1898. Meeting
opened by singing “Come let us anew.”
Prayer by Brother James Maxwell. Sing-
ing “Now let us rejoice in the day of salva-
tion.” Counselor Martha B. Taylor pre-
sided.
	Elder L. John Nuttall offered some pre-
liminary remarks relative to the object and
purpose of the organization of the Relief
Society as contemplated by the Prophet
Joseph Smith, and referred to the manner
of keeping accounts so as to consume as lit-
tle time as possible when assembled together
in meeting. Said while the distribution
of donations was an important part. still
there were higher motives and objects to be
attained by meeting together. We should
have lectures in the Society and endeavor to
use the time profitably, other than a few
bearing practically the same testimony over
and over again.
	Sister M. Isabella Horne, president of
the Relief Society of Salt Lake Stake, made
some very excellent remarks relative to the
purpose and organization of the Relief Soci-
ety, and said that the young as well as the
elderly people should associate themselves
with the Society.
	Bishop M. S. Woolley said he was
pleased to meet with the sisters this after-
noon, and that the Society was a great as-
sistance to the Bishops in caring for the
poor and encouraged all to go on with the
work.
	Sister Ann O. Burt tendered her resigna-
tion as president and director of the 21st
ward Relief Society, the same was read by
Bishop M. S. Woolley. On motion the resig-
nation was unanimously accepted. On
motion Sister Frances L. Castleton was
elected a director of the Society.
	Bishop M. S. Woolley read the resigna-
tion of Sister Annie M. Reynolds as director
and secretary of the Society, which was un-
animously accepted. On motion Sister
Mary A. Tuddenham was elected a director.
	Sister Jane Mulholland tendered her

[Column 2]
verbal resignation as director and treasurer.
On motion the resignation was unanimously
accepted. On motion Sister Helen V.
Lyon was elected a director.
	The following names were suggested by
Bishop M. S. Woolley: Eliza Barton as
president; Martha B. Taylor as first counsel-
or; Frances L. Castleton as second counsel-
or; Mary A. Tuddenham as secretary;
Helen V. Lyon as treasurer. On motion
the above named persons were unanimous-
ly sustained.
	Sister Annie T. Hyde spoke relative to
the mission and organization of the Soci-
ety as contemplated by the Prophet Joseph
Smith, and solciited the attendance of the
younger and middle aged women at the
meetings. Made mention of the fact that
Sister Eliza R. Snow was about thirty-
eight years of age when the Society was or-
ganized by the prophet.
	President Joseph E. Taylor said he had
been invited by the Bishop to attend this
meeting as there was contemplated changes
in the Society, and feeling an interest in all
the branches was present. He had been
called upon on several occasions to adjust
differences that existed between the Bishop
and officers of some branches of the Relief
Society. Thought the sisters should be
given all the liberty possible io magnify
their office. The sisters in return should
gather the strength of the Priesthood and
be in harmony with the Bishop and counsel-
ors of the ward. Cautioned the sisters in
their meetings relative to the gift of tongues
and thought we should confine ourselves
within the proper limits and seek for the
Spirit of God in our gatherings.
	Counselor H. J. Foulger referred to the
past labors of Sister Ann O Burt as presi-
dent, the difficulties the sisters met with
when first organized, and the means em-
ployed by her and Sister Allen in raising a
store on the block to benefit the Society.
Moved that a hearty vote of thanks be
tendered her for her past labors, and that
the blessing of the Almighty might rest up-
on her.
	A vote of thanks was also tendered Sis-
ters Reynolds and Mulholland for their
past efficient labors in the Society, all of
which were unanimous. The Doxology
was sung and meeting closed with benedic-
tion by Bishop M. S. Woolley.
	Meeting at the residence of Sister Eliza
Barton at 5.30 p. m. Sister Barton through
illness was unable to attend the meeting at
the Society hall.

ed in St. Louis. The offices will be rented
to business women or to people having busi-

	interest four times a year.

		WILFORD WOODRUFF, Prest.
		GEORGE M. CANNON, Cashier.
