For the Exponent.
SPRINGTIME.

AH! my love, come here.
Winter's breaking spring is waking,
Hark! a lark sings clear;
From the lakes the flocks are taking
Flight to northward, and I hear
’Neath the ice the ripples making
Music to the ear.
Soon the green will sprin[-],
Where the snow lay shall be pathway,
Sweet with blossoming;
From the glens a chorus ringing,
Will bear outward and away,
Message sweet in echoes singing
Of the coming May.
And the days shall gain 
Each one faire, bringing rarer 
Gifts than flowers that throw
Perfume to the winds, their bearer;
Colors, odors, changing so
Into gifts to each glad sharer—
Autumn’s harvests show.
So, for human hearts
From beginning, still is winging 
Where each lifeway starts,
From Above (sweeter than singing)
Flight to earthward—nothing parts  
Or can turn the angel, bringing
Help to waiting hearts. 
And a voice calls “Here.”
Earth-bands breaking, joy is waking—
Hark!  the song draws near—
From the death-clasp souls are taking
Flight up heavenward and they hear
O'er earth’s mourners angels making,
Welcomes sweet and clear. 
	AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
Salt Lake City, March 31, 1898.


[Column 1]
LIFE’S PILGRIMAGE. 
‘Is life worth the living?” the millionaire said,
As he rose in the morn from his soft, downy bed,
In his palace of grandeur surrounded by wealth, 
And all that could add to his comfort or health–
Yet he oft-times exclaims “Is life worth the liv-
	ing?”
“Is life worth the living?” the laborer asks
As he trudges along to his wearisome tasks,
The beauties of nature spread out to his sight,
He is blind to these charms: he feels no delight,
And says in his soul “Life’s not worth the living.”

[Column 2]
“Is life worth the living?” the poor pauper cries—
With cravings of hunger and deep smothered 
	sighs,
So little to cheer him, so many desires, 
Neglected and weary he often inquires—
Poor broken spirit! “Is life worth the living?”

“Is life worth the living?” the epicure yawns,
While the sycophant near him insipidly fawns
With satiated tastes and o’er-clouded brains,
They drink and carouse, on their ill-gotten gains,
Ah, well one may ask “Are such lives worth
	living?”

‘Life’s not worth the living,” the pessamist thinks,
As he finds to what depths humanity sinks,
The dark, cloudy side is all that he sees, 
Pain, sickness and sorrow and mental disease,
So sagely concludes “Life’s not worth the liv-
	ing.”

“But life is worth living,” the optimist writes,
Every hour is giving the sweetest delights,
this beautiful world! has its sunshine and 
	flowers;
And science and truth are increasing the powers
Of brain, heart and soul: True! life’s worth the
	living.”

“This world is so pretty!” the little child lisps,
As she smells the new hay and plays with its 
	wisps,
The songs of the birds, and flowers are sweet,
And the green grass is cool and soft for my feet,
While God up in Heaven these blessings is giv-
	ing.”

“Life is worth living!” the youth now exclaims,
As he thinks of the future and makes lofty aims;
“I’ll try to be good and noble and great,
Prove faithful and true to my second estate, 
And make the world better because of my living.”

“Life is worth living!” says the maiden in bliss,
As she bends her fair head for her lover’s first
	kiss.
“The earth is o’er spread with roseate hue,
The world is all love, the sky is so blue!
And ne’er did life seem so much worth the
	living!”

“Life is worth living!” says husband and wife, 
As they walk hand in hand the journey of life; 
“We’ll share every sorrow and joy as they come,
We will try to bring sunshine into our home, 
And rear sons and daughters worthy of living.”

“Yes life is worth living!" the mother replies, 
As she looks in the depths of her baby’s brown 
	eyes.
“I must polish this jewel and make it so bright 
That ’twill shine to the world with radiant light,
And make all men feel that life is worth living.”

‘Life is worth living!” are the words that he gave
As he tottered so near to the brink of the grave. 
“I’ve lived ninety years, seen life’s ups and downs,
But with all of its turmoil, its cares and its frowns
I continue to think that life is worth living.”

Ah, live’s worth the living! though drear it of
	seems,
And our seasons of joy are like fast fleeling
	dreams.
The infinite bliss of a being on earth;
With the exquisite hope of a Celestial birth!
Help us bear all its cares and make life worth
	the living. 

[Column 3]
“Yes life is worth living," the Christian declares
“With all of its trials, its sins and its snares,
For through stern experience wisdom is gained, 
A triumph o’er self and the world is obtained, 
And mankind are secured a life everlasting.”
						ELLIS R. SHIPP.


NOTICE TO THOSE INTERESTED IN
        SILK CULTURE IN UTAH.
   THE Utah Silk Commission desires to
call attention to the fact that the time for
raising silk is again upon us, and we trust
that those who intend entering upon the
work this year will write to us at once for
eggs. Those who are taking up the work
for the first time will kindly state the num-
ber and size of the trees from which they
can obtain leaves; also the number of per-
sons who can give their time during the
latter period of the raising season, so that
we will be able to send the necessary
amount of eggs.
   We also desire to state that eggs can be
obtained from the Commission free, and we
would prefer everyone who is going to
raise the worms to have seed from us as we
provide a quality which gives a much bet-
ter result than those raised by people hav-
ing little or no experience. We will send
pamphlet of instruction free to those wish-
ing any information upon the subject.
   We trust that many people throughout
the State have already planted numbers of
mulberry trees, and have been watchful lest
any of those which have grown are in dan-
ger of being destroyed. We also wish to
say to those who have trees and do not care
to use them, that they may do a great deal
towards establishing this important industry
by allowing others to use the leaves, and
we assure you that it will only benefit the
trees, even if they are stripped several times
they will soon become much more thrifty,
and this will also be a means of disposing
of the fruit which is sometimes an annoy-
ance, especially when trees are planted on
the street.
   As a whole the Commission feels very
much encouraged with the result of last
year’s work, and trust it will be very much
increased this year. Address all communi-
cations to
		MARGARET A. CAINE,
	Secretary Utah Silk Commission.
				Salt Lake City Utah.


FRANCES WILLARD wrote to her friend
Mrs. Bain of Kentucky, who had just lost a
daughter: "Dear sister Ann, how much
richer are you than I! Here I sit alone
without a child to die, while you are moth-
er to an angel."
THE principal women thus far mentioned
as candidates for the presidency of the Gen-
eral Federation of Women’s Clubs, to suc-
ceed Mrs. Henrotin, are Mrs. Alice Ives
Breed, of Massachusetts; Mrs. Sarah S.
Platt, of Colorado; Mrs. William Tod
Helmuth, of New York; Mrs. Edwin
Longstreth, of Pennsylvania, and Mrs.
Susan Riley Ashley, of Colorado.


WOMANS EXPONENT


[column 1]
HOW TO BUILD A GRANARY ON
NOTHING.

	THE St. George Relief Society was in a
quandary. Conference had closed and for
the thousandth time the subject of the stor-
ing of grain had been discussed. But this
time the Spirit had seemed more urgent
than ever before. With an omnious war-
cloud hanging over our very heads; with
tales of suffering and starvation of the most
appaling nature in various parts of our little
earth being brought to us daily; with late
frosts nipping the tender buds of our fruit
trees right at home, the theme refused to be
folded away in its accustomed napkin to
await the regular quarterly resurrection.
There it hovered, outlined in bold relief
against a background of foretold want and
misery and refused to be downed. 
	But what was to be done? An inventory
of the resources of the Society revealed half
a dozen patch-work quilts, some finished,
others in course of construction; a few pairs
of partly finished stockings and socks, a lim-
ited quantity of carpet rags, and a very
small amount of credit at the various indus-
trial and manufacturing concerns of the
country.
	“But we’ve got to have a granary,” said
the officers. “There is no getting around
it. It haunts us night and day, and we
cannot afford to have our health ruined by
insomnia,” so they put their heads to-
gether. The result was a committee.
What that committee was about was for a
time a secret. But it finally leaked out that
we were to have a “Bee.” It was not to
be a rag bee or a quilting, but a “Bee” to
which all our husbands and brothers and
sons were to be invited. They were not to
sip tea and knit socks for Sam, Dick and
Harry, but were to bring themselves, their
wagons and teams, gather the material and
build a granary.
	A day was set, and such a response! At
least one hundred and seventy-five men an
boys, with twenty-five good teams and 
wagons made lively times in the vicinity of
the Lyceum. There being a great many
more than could be utilized on the granary
a brigade was organized to clear up the
streets and renovate crossings, etc., and
another to devote themselves to fixing up
the Tabernacle grounds. Such a spirit of
unity and good feeling as prevailed among
them is seldom witnessed. Every one
seemed desirous of doing somebody else a
good turn, and seemed the happier when-
ever a slight circumstance or accident rend-
ered assistance necessary.
	During this time the ladies of the popu-
lace were not idle, as the following very
tempting menu will testify. As one of the
committee remarked, “We seated one hun-
dred and fifty men at dinner, and fed the
five thousand in the evening.” “And did
you gather up ten basketsfull after the
feast?” I asked. “I really believe we did,”
was the reply.
	Menu: Chickens roasted and boiled,
sixteen; baked beans, sixteen pans; toma-
toes, ten quarts; bread, forty-six loaves;
butter, twenty pounds; salmon, three cans;
eggs fried, thirty dozen; rice puddings,
seven; beef, three large joints; ham, three
large dishes; potatoes, thirty pounds; pick-
les, eight quarts; milk, eight quarts; sugar,
twenty-five pounds; pies, one hundred and
ten; cakes, fifty-four; fruits, thirty-two
quarts; tarts ad infinitum.
	In addition to the above, various articles
were given in which could not be used at

[Column 2]
the time, but which will come in useful
in the future. Quite a little sum was
also realized in cash, store pay, tithing pay
and factory pay, all of which will assist in
the good work.
	Now as to the labor accomplished:
Masonry about				$115,00.
Rock hauling, 96 perches		57.50.
Clay, 10 loads					5.00.
Sand						12.00.

Total work on granary			$189.50.
	In addition to the above thirty-five loads
of shale were hauled into the Tabernacle
yard, nearly as many more at street cross-
ings and a number of loads of rock for the
new school building in course of construc-
tion. All this, with the good work of the
street crossing committee would foot up a
grand total, could we arrive at the exact
figures.
	An entertainment was tendered the gen-
tlemen, their wives and sweethearts, in the
evening in the theatre. A nice program
was rendered, tempting refreshments served
after which the remainder of the time was
spent pleasantly in dancing.
	Dear Aunt Em and the readers of the
“Exponent” pardon this long contribution.
I have given the particulars thus in detail
in the hope that it might inspire some
others having similiar desires, but a deficien-
cy of the wherewithal, to go forth and do
likewise, with this example of successful
effort before them.
	Our granary will have a capacity of two
thousand bushels, and we expect to have it
ready to receive the wheat at harvest time.
Our success has made us bold, and we will
be pardoned for asserting that we do not
anticipate any trouble in getting grain to
put into it.
	We trust that the end and aim of this
communication may speedily be realized in
learning of some other struggling Society
making an effort in this necessary direction.
			JULIA A. PACE, Cor. Sec.
	St. George Stake.


A RAMBLING LETTER.

	WINTER is beginning to wane and the
farmers are commencing to prepare for seed
time. The school land is in the market,
and our friends and relatives are coming
here to make their homes and fortunes in
this land of “Uncle Sam.” The boom-
ers land is all that seems to be lying idle
now; perhaps when all the rest is improved
this will sell to someone who needs a home.
	We had a good attendance at our Relief
Society conference, which was held at Idaho
Falls on the 13th of last month. The house
was comfortably filled, especially in the
afternoon, although it was blowing a
very keen wind from the south. The new
meeting house is a very nice edifice and
does credit to the ward. I hope we will be
as successful at Willow Creek. We received
some good instructions from the presidency
of the Stake, also from Sister Bennett and
counselors, our lady presidency of the Stake
of Bingham. Our young people are looking
forward to a treat, their conference coming
on the 16th of this month. We are very
proud of this Association. Our officers both
of the Stake and of the ward are efficient in
their abilities and energy. We parents are
glad to see the young people growing up to
be useful and trying to learn the principles
of the Gospel, which they do when they

[Column 2]
are interested in their Mutual Improvement
Associations. We rejoice that we are in
possession of a religion that will make a
happy people.
	The District Court is in session at this
time, and as usual divorces are being called
for and granted. It is alarming, I think,
that so many divorces seem to be needed.
I hope that our young people will, and I
believe they do, appreciate the plain counsel
they are getting; truly the ax is being laid
at the root of the tree. I believe one cause
why so much petty jealousy and unpleasant-
ness arises in so many families commences
through lack of confidence in one another;
and why this lack of confidence ? If we
would trace it back sometimes we would find
it was because of undue liberty before mar-
riage; and therefore lack of confidence in
one another leads to disrespect and disre-
spect to hate, hence the only way they can
see out is to get divorced, and often this is
“jumping out of the frying pan into the
fire.”
	Are we profiting by the many sermons
we hear? A woman who was born in the
Church but had married into an apostate
family, said to me one day that she could
see little difference in church going people
or in their children who attended Sabbath
School, and those who did not; she failed to
see any difference in those who prayed or
those that were taught to pray, and those
who did not. Her children, she said, were
just as good as anybody’s, and they did not
pray. I told her that we who attended
meetings and prayed did not profess to be
perfect., that the Church was a school and
some of us are very dull and slow to learn
it is true, and profit very little by attending
meetings perhaps; but there are two natures
in mankind, human and divine: “To err is
human; to forgive divine.” Those who
pray cultivate the divine nature, they can
forgive. I said to her “You are not happy
when you treasure up some trifle which
you imagine is so bad that a neighbor or
neighbor’s child has done or said. You
may sometimes forgive one who believes as
you do, but one who is trying to be a Saint
you cannot forgive, you do not seek the
spirit of forgiveness.” She said, “Well I
am satisfied, I would not have my children
blessed nor baptized, because I will not
allow religion to interfere when their father
does not believe in it. He is a good pro-
vider and that is all I want.” “Well,” I
said, “we will not enjoy this life always
and we must prepare for an eternity. I
want my children to go to Sabbath School,
also to be baptized and confirmed, that they
may be able to cultivate, if they are dili-
gent, the higher nature, this is the object,
I believe, of all religious meetings whether
of individuals, associations, Sabbath School
meetings or meetings of worship; they are
for the cultivation of our higher natures. If
the partakers of all these benefits are not
any better for them it is not the fault of the
Church nor the teachers in the Church, but
those who are idle.”
	That we may still work for the building
up of the kingdom is the desire of
						GRANDMA.
	Willow Creek, Bingham, Idaho.


"EMPRESS AUGUSTA VICTORIA, of Ger-
many, found 144 German servant girls last

WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


I HEARD the gentle murmur
   Of voices strange and sweet;
And the indistinct low music 
   Of hosts of marching feet.
And then I heard the murmur
   Of a thousand voices say;
“All hail sweet sacred morning!
   Rejoice! ’tis Easter day?
Awake! awake, O nature!
   O joy-bells loudly ring!
Awake for Christ is risen,
   And praise is due our King”
			ELIZABETH A. VORE.


SLEEP WELL BRAVE HEART.

	[Written while Mrs. Dickinson sat by the side
of Miss Willard’s casket Monday, Feb 21, 1898.]
SLEEP well, brave heart, beloved of Christ and
	crowned,
		He gives thee sleep.
The wide world’s love enwraps thy slumber round,
		God gives thee sleep.
His angels smile; His stricken children weep;
Yet smiles, nor tears, shall break thy blessed
	sleep.
Oh, wondrous face! whose mystic, solemn grace
		O’er-floods the gloom,
Till grief—in all this sorrow-haunted place—
		No more finds room.
Show us, dear God, what glory greets her eyes,
Let us, too, hear the voice that bids her rise.
Chide not our tears! So weak we are and blind,
		Yet she would share 
Her gladness with us who are left behind.
		Heed Thou our prayer!
Not yet. Not yet? Tarries the vision still?
Then, grant us, Lord—with her—to love Thy will
To work Thy will, to follow where she trod, 
		Without a fear;
To drink her cup; to climb the heights of God
		Knowing her near;
To make her joy more joyful by our strife.
So may we share even here her glorious life.
So may our homes, our land in shame so long, 
		Be cleansed from wrong;
So may our breaking hearts through love be 
	strong,
		Through love be strong.
Bless still, through us, earth’s sinning and op-
	pressed.
Thy work rests not, dear heart. Take thou the
	rest.
			MARY LOWE DICKINSON,
—Union Signal


[Column 1]
MRS. E. B. WELLS.

Published by request.
	THE pleasing duty has been assigned to
me to write a few thoughts of my esteemed 
friend Mrs. E. B. Wells. Behold this gather-
ing! Women associated in two clubs, the
Press and the Reapers, whose organizer she
is. Here are women of intelligence, poets, 
writers and thinkers, whose common aim is
to elevate themselves, to understand and
further develop the God given talents given
to them.
	On each one she bestows personal care
and encouragement. Each one can tell of
many times when by her counsel she has
been strengthened, and the resolution
formed to make another effort.
	Editor of the “Exponent” for many
years, her words are read the world over.
Its columns are open to the women of Utah
irrespective of nationality or creed. Being
the organ of the Relief Society one of the larg-
est organizations of women in the world,

[Column 2]
Mrs. Wells its secretary—the labor per-
formed by the women of Mormondom is
outlined. All may read the page. The
publication referred to is an advocate of the
principles of truth. Gems of thought both
prose and poetry adorn it.
	This noble woman after spending a life-
time in aiding the poor, relieving their dis-
tress and speaking the comforting word to
those bowed down in sorrow, at the age of
seventy years gives to the world her book
of choice poems.
	“Musings and Memories” just issued
from the press is the crowning effort of her
life. It is replete with beautiful thoughts,
spun in threads of light and sparkling with
rare intelligence. Many of the poems con-
tain sublime pathos combined with sweet
simplicity. I will mention “At Last.”

“There must be something we desire to gain,
	A recompense for pacing to and fro,
Some sweet fruition which we would obtain,
	Or greater wisdom that we fain would know.
And this is why we toil on and endure—
	Do battle ’mid life’s trials, storms and strife,
Because we feel that there is something sure
	That we shall find, within a higher life.”

	Thoughts reaching out to the majesty on
high in “Meditation.”

“My soul has gone forth in its wandering, 
	To the hills that are purple with light;
Those temples that tower everlasting,
	In their majesty, grandeur and might.

But the life giving means is the marvel,
	The mystery, past knowledge and skill;
No man can the secret unravel;
	It abideth in God at His will.”

	Whisperings of a pre-existence is awak-
ened in “Shadows and Whisperings.”

“I sometimes think I must have lived before,
	In a much brighter fairer world than this;
That I have stepped from that enchanted shore,
	As though I woke from out a dream of bliss,
Where soft hands lingered in a fond caress
	Upon my head, to soothe me and to bless.”

	A life beyond the grave is shadowed in
“Thoughts and Memories of a Summer
Hour.”

“Tears fall and mingle with the pearly dew,
	And flowers spring up afresh where all was drear;
And something whispers of a life more true,
	Where perfect love will reign devoid of fear.”

	Comforting words to the weary heart,
faint unto weeping and worn with many
prayers, are sung to the “Bereaved Mother.”

E’en if weary and broken hearted,
	With that which thou deemest loss;
Although from the dearest ones parted,
	He will help thee bear thy cross;
And if the ordeal seems cruel,
	The Father will make it right,
And the brighter thy precious jewel,
	Will shine in the realms of light.”

	The beauties of nature are extolled. One
thinks of the beautiful earth clothed in ver-
dure, arrayed in flowers, with singing
birds, oceans grand and forest shades.

“O, how we yearn to understand still more
	The mysteries that nature has concealed;
The knowledge of her science—hidden lore,
	Which unto none of us has been revealed.
Nor will it be till we have proved how true
	Our trust has been in what we came to do.”

	How sweet is sympathy! a slender gold-
en chain it links suffering hearts together.
Tears of sorrow touched the Master’s heart 

[Column 3]
whose chords vibrated at sight of human
woe, and Jesus wept. From “Sorrow and
Sympathy.”

“And as we do to others, in their need,
	Loving our neighbor as the Savior taught;
Then we are sowing broadcast precious seed,
	And we shall reap a harvest where we’ve
	wrought;
For if our faith fail not and we endure,
Then all our Father’s promises are sure.” 

	Love the best thing in the world, all
powerful, everlasting and enduring in
“Autumnal Musings.”

“The harp strings may be shattered,
And the rose leaves may be scattered,
	But the memory still remaineth;
And the love that we have cherished,
And the hope that we have nourished,
	The immortal soul retaineth.”

	How beautiful are spiritual thoughts!
So near to the gates of glory that stray
beams shine through the golden bars, and
mingling with the perfumed air, strains of
softest melody float over the purple hills of
Paradise. They soothe the aching heart
and throbbing brain. O, just over there is
a “Home Immortal.”

“No more weeping, no more sighing,
	No more agonizing fears;
And no requiem for the dying;
	Chanted ’mid the falling tears.

Sweetest strains of music ringing,
	Echo through the wide domain,
Choirs of heavenly voices singing,
	“Nevermore to part again.’”

	Some lives go out in glory. Like the
sun blazing as it sinks below the horizon,
casting its reflection far over the heavens of
burning ships heaving and tossing, flaming
chariots moving slowly along and seas of
somber blue bordered by fleecy clouds edged
with gleams of gold. The mountains catch
the radiance, and high up their sides wide
valleys appear, even deep ravines are gilded
by the grand effulgence of the sun’s last
gorgeous rays.
	Well may this, our friend, feel proud of
her life’s work, and many will rise up and
call her blessed. On history’s page her
name is written in letters of gold.
	May her heart be comforted, her head
rest lightly on her pillow, her beautiful faith
in God our Father constantly increase.
	Mrs. Wells, accept the love and congratu-
lations of your friends, who join in wishing
you many happy returns of your natal day,
and in saying “The world is better for
your having lived in it.”
				LYDIA D. ALDER.


MRS. MANNING, of Albany, N. Y., the
new president of the National Society of
the Daughters of the American Revolution,
was born Mary Margaretta Fryer, and her
ancestors on her father’s side were Dutch.
On her mother’s side she goes back to
Robert Livingston, first Lord of the manor
of Livingston, to whom was given the or-
iginal grant. Among her more famous an-
cestors are Philip Livings’on, the second
lord; Col. Peter R. Livingston, Governor
Rip Van Dam, Abraham de Peyster. Olaff
Stevenson Van Courtlandt and Col. Peter
Schuyler. Her late husband, Daniel Man-
ning, was secretary of the treasury during
Cleveland’s first administration. Mrs.
Manning entertained with much grace dur-
ing her residence in Washington, and her
house in Albany, is one of the stateliest in
that city.						Ex.

SALT LAKE CITY APRIL 15, 1898.


THE APRIL CONFERENCE.

	THE General Conference of the Church
which commenced on April 6, and closed
its session on April 10, Sunday afternoon,
was one of the best, if not the very best
conference ever held in this valley. The
Spirit of God was specially manifest to all
who were present and the teachings and in-
struction given were received with joy and
gladness by the assembled multitudes. Cer-
tainly the Saints were fed with the bread of
life.
	The music and singing was of the most
exalted character, uplifting to the soul and
in harmony with the entire spirit that pre-
vailed throughout these sacred gatherings,
for it was indeed like a continued Sabbath
	The testimonies of prophets and apostles
were powerful and impressive and must
carry conviction of truth and earnestness to
the hearts of those who listened to the
words of divine inspiration.
	President Woodruff at the advanced age
of ninety-one years standing in the great
congregation and bearing testimony day
after day and time after time was a wonder-
ful manifestation of spiritual power, in that
he not only was able to make the people
hear in that great edifice, but his ideas were
more clearly and forcibly presented than
ever, if that were possible. The last after-
noon of the Sabbath day was so rich in in-
spirational teaching applicable to the times
and condition of the people, that one could
not but feel vividly the presence of heaven-
ly beings, even though they may not
have been seen by mortal eyes. Certainly
the power of the Holy Ghost was percept-
ble to thousands of witnesses.


"NO MAN KNOWETH."

	IN the midst of the excitement that pre-
vails not only in the United States and the
Congress thereof but elsewhere throughout
many foreign countries, one cannot help
feeling how uncertain the near future is to
the world of mankind. At one moment
war seems imminent and perhaps inevitable
then there seems a lull and a tendency to-
wards peace negotiations, and before quiet
can settle more news comes of commotion
and turmoil and more outrages in Cuba,
and it would indeed seem "the world is
turning upside down," if not quite turned.
Any one with a prophetic nature must be
forcibly reminded of predictions concerning
the war that have been foretold would take
place in the last days, and also calamities of


various kinds, and therefore those who be-
lieve these things are watching carefully
the signs of the times and are praying
earnestly that the Lord will over-rule all
things according to His divine will and to
further His purposes in the earth.
	It is ardently to be desired that the Lat-
ter day Saints follow the counsel of the ser-
vants of the Lord in all these matters and
surely the counsel given at the recent con-
ference ought to be a guide to those who
are anxious to keep the commandments of 
God. The Saints know that they shoul
refrain from all evil and wicked thoughts
and desires, but if called to go to battle for
their country they would undoubtedly be
as ready to stand by the stars and stripes as
any men in the United States. The “Mor-
mon” people have never been accused of cow-
ardice, and they have proved in times past
that the call of their country should be
obeyed whatever might be the circumstanc
es, as in the call when the Nauvoo battalion
was fitted out at a time of great emergency
when traveling from Nauvoo across the
country five hundred men left their famil-
ies homeless, to go to Mexico. This event
has passed into history. But for the sake of
humanity it is to be hoped that peace will
spread her white wings over the country
and that under the shadow of her wings
Cuba may find refuge from war and blood-
shed, famine and disease and all the attend-
ant evils that follow a relentless and blood-
thirsty foe. 
	Yet no man knoweth what shall be the
consequence of these uprisings, and the ex-
citement that prevails at the present time
and will not seemingly be settled by arbi-
tration or armistice.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	MADAME MOUNTFORD has arrived at
Jerusalem and is at present quartered at
the Howard Hotel. She says in her mes-
sage to the writer, March 24, that she has
not forgotten Zion and desires to be remem-
bered to all inquiring friends.
	THE Welsh prize singers gave their con-
cert in the large Tabernacle early in the
month. Judging from current opinions the
affair was a greal musical success. It seems
to be quite popular to give concerts, lectures
and such like entertainments in the great
“Mormon” Tabernacle.
	MME. MELBA and her grand opera com-
pany arrived in the city last night and will
appear in the large Tabernacle tonight. The
Tabernacle choir will also take part in the
concert to be given. Mme. Melba is an
Australian and takes her stage name from
the city of Melbourne where she was born.
Her bird-like voice has made her famous the
world over. Of her personality or history
very little seems to be known by local peo-
ple in Salt Lake City.


GENERAL RELIEF SOCIETY
CONFERENCE.

	THE General Conference of the Relief
Society was held in the Salt Lake Assembly
Hall, Tuesday, April 5, 1898. Meeting
commenced at 10 a. m. Mrs. Zina D. H.
Young presiding. Her counselors, Jane S.
Richards and Bathsheba W. Smith were
present, also the secretary, E. B. Wells, and
treasurer, M. Isabella Horne and a number

[Column 2]
of Stake presidents of  the organization.
Meeting opened by singing. “How firm a
foundation.” Prayer by President F. A.
Hammond of San Juan Stake. Singing
“Praise to the man” etc.
	President Zina D. H. Young made an
address of loving welcome to the sisters,
referred to the first organization of the Soci-
ety and stated that many prophecies uttered
then had already come to pass, which gave
us courage to believe that other predictions
would be literally fulfilled. She felt proud
of the daughters of Zion, reminded the con-
gregation that example was better than
precept; we had been blest abundantly
both spiritually and temporally, she thought
if Zion was to be the joy of the whole
world the sisters must have part in it, for a
great part of the world were females. She 
would like to hear the reports of those from
a distance.
	The secretary called the roll of Stakes
and twenty-seven responded. Minutes of
the October conference read and approved.
	President Jane S. Richards was glad we
could assemble in peace and love and medi-
tate on things spiritual when there was so
much stir in the world and talk of war and
so many changes taking place around us,
yet we had no confusion and could rejoice
in the Gospel and try to serve the Lord.
She often wondered if we were living to ob-
tain the promises made to those who were
true and faithful and sought to keep the
commandments given to the Saints. Ex-
horted mothers to be full of love and chari-
ty and kindness, referred to happiness in
the home, what a beautiful thing it was to
look back upon, and for herself it was a
great pleasure to recall her home with her
father and mother. Speaking of kind words
she recalled when she was coming out of
Nauvoo there was a brother who used to
speak kind words to her, when she was
very sick and felt as if she had more than
she could bear, it was a very great comfort
to her. Sister Richards urged the sisters to
pray for and sustain the president of the
Relief Society, Sister Zina D. H. Young,
“who needs your faith and who has not been
very well of late, and may we as sisters be
able to do all that will be for our salvation is
my prayer.”
	Sister B. W. Smith after some preliminary
remarks said we had come here to keep our
second estate if we can keep every command-
ment, observe the laws and regulations of
our holy religion, we should be kind and
good and encourage each other, setting
good examples and putting away the love
of riches and lay up treasures where moth
and rust doth not corrupt. Sister Smith
stated her time was mostly spent in the
Temple, she did not go out much to speak
to the sisters but met a great many in the
Temple constantly and she loved them.
“We know this work is true, we must try
and attend to our prayers, be patient and
just, set our minds to do something worthy.
We used to have Brother Joseph to teach
us, he said on one occasion he would not be
with us long and in his prayer at that time
his voice shook.” She would be glad to see
every Society have a house of its own, dedi-
cated on purpose, the brethren ought to as-
sist us, we have helped them so much, we
should have unison in all these things. “It
would be well if our children could realize
what we have passed through for the Gos-
pel, and the Gospel will be more to us than
riches that we may obtain what we desire,
that we may have homes to receive those


who are in need—there will be calamities
and war and famine and sickness, may
every blessing rest upon us in the name of
the Lord, amen.” 
	President M. A. Hyde, Sanpete Stake,
said “I don’t blame the sisters for being
diffident in standing up here, it is hard for
me. I will speak my feelings about a
house, and then when we come from all
parts of the State that there will be a re-
sponse all around. I attended conference
at Ephraim—Sisters Wells and Annie T.
Hyde were with us—the sisters in that
Stake are very industrious and devise many
ways and means to help build up the Soci-
ety.,’ Sister Hyde felt greatly exercised
about the youth of Zion, spoke of some
painful circumstances, the adversary is
evidently doing his part and we should do
ours; rejoiced that there were so many
present and hoped ere long we might meet
in our own house. Sister Hyde then spoke
a few words in the gift of tongues interpret-
ed by Sister Zina D. H. Young.
	President F. A. Hammond of San Juan
Stake, represented the Relief Society in the
unavoidable absence of the president: in the
course of his remarks he said, “I make it a
rule of my life to attend the conferences of
the Church and have always been blest in
attending. I don’t believe I ever have lost
a cent by coming.” Spoke well of the Re-
lief Society in his Stake, felt that the Soci-
ety was one of the strongest helps that the
Bishops had, the sisters not only pour in
the oil and the wine when any thing is
wanted but in other ways are helpful. “I
have been a strong advocate for the sisters
these many years, I take pleasure in their
labors, I have looked forward to the time
when the sisters would be sent on missions,
Sister Jennie Brimhall and Sister Lulu
Knight have been set apart and are now on
their way to England, our sisters will be
sent off to the nations of the earth and they
should have a knowledge for themselves.
I have advocated the education of our girls,
for this reason I am in favor of Church
schools. Speaking of a house for our sis-
ters I feel it is a proper thing, and it should
be a house dedicated for the purpose, there
is not a ward or Stake house where the sis-
ters have not aided; the Bishops throughout
Zion should help build a house for the Re-
lief Society. Sisters my faith is you will
succeed, a house where the general author-
ities would hold and arrange the affairs of
the Society. I feel to bless Aunt Zina and
her counselors, I have known them for
years, this is my prayer in the name of
Jesus.”
	Singing, Doxology. Benediction by
President M. Isabella Horne.
				E. B. WELLS, Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


U. W. P. C.

	THE ladies of the U. W. P. C. met ac-
cording to adjournment Thursday evening,
March 31, 1898. Club was called to order
by President Romania B. Pratt. Prayer by 
Mrs. Lydia D. Alder. Roll call with re-
sponse from Spanish authors. Under un-
finished business the question of issuing re-
ceipts for membership fees was discussed.
A motion in its favor by Mrs. Emily S.
Richards wascarried. A motion was made
and carried that the club subscribe
$5.00 from the treasury for charter mem-
bership in the George Washington Memor-
ial Association, was carried.


In the General Council of Woman’s Clubs
the Press Club was given the appointment
of secretary from its membership. A letter
was read from Ruth M. Fox contain
ing a statement to that effect and a motion
by Mrs. E. B. Wells was carried to the ef-
fect that President Pratt make the appoint-
ment after time for deliberation.
	Mrs. Lydia D. Alder read an invitation
given by the Inqueires Club to the Press
Club to attend a lecture by Rev. Alfred
Henry, to be given Monday April 4th, at
2:30 p. m. at the M. E. Church. In behalf
of the club Mrs. Alder accepted the invita-
tion by letter.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells proposed the following
names for acceptance as honorary members:
Mrs. Emily H.Woodmansee, Mrs. Maria Mil-
ler Johnson and Mrs. Augusta Joyce
Crocheron. Motion was seconded and car-
ried.
	Mrs. Emily S. Richards then read a very
ably written paper on Portugal followad by
Mrs. E. B. Wells with a beautiful poem “Is
life worth the living?” written by Dr. Ellis
R. Shipp. A short and interesting sketch
of Spain was given in a well written paper
by Mrs. Phebe C. Young. Dr. Orielle Curtis
dealt with Cuba in written words, glowing,
stirring and eloquent.
	Program presented for April 30, as fol-
lows: Paper on Frances E. Willard, E. B.
Wells; Zolo the French author, Helen V.
Kimball; Poem, Lydia D. Alder; Alfred
Austin, England’s Poet Laureate, Edith
Lovsey; sentiments from noted American
women.
	Critics report given, minutes of meeting
read and approved and dismissed with prayer.
	At the close of the meeting a
a dainty lunch was served through the hos-
pitable courtesy of President R. B. Pratt
and Mrs. E. B. Wells, and a social half
hour was agreeably spent.
			M. A. J. LAMBERT, Sec.


A BIRTHDAY PARTY.

	MARCH 4, was the birthday of Sister
Sarah S. Forsyth, second counselor to Sis-
ter Jane S. Coleman, president of the Relief
Society of the Wayne Stake of Zion. And
inasmuch as Sister Forsyth expected to move
away from among us, and her labors in the
Society had been very earnest, a birthday
surprise was gotten up for her at the resi-
dence of Sister Margaret J. Taylor, presi-
dent of the Relief Society of the Loa ward.
Many of her friends there expressed to her
their appreciation of her labors among the
sick, and the kindness and love she had
shown to the poor and oppressed.
	An excellent dinner was served, after
partaking of which presents were made us
mementoes and keepsakes. Songs and
hymns were sung, and many strong testi-
monies were borne to the truth of the Gos-
pel, and in behalf of Sister Forsyth, kind and
loving speeches were made and her chari-
table works, kind deeds and devotion to
the truth were spoken of. Among the
many who were present were Sister Lucinda
Brown, the oldest inhabitant of Loa Ward,
Margaret Pace and Nancy Blackburn, who
have also passed through the early trials of
the Latter-day Saints. “When shall we all
meet again” was sung to close with, and the
following verses were prepared and presented
to Sister Sarah Forsyth. 
	Tis forty-six years since the day of thy birth,
	Thy spirit untarnished then came to the earth,


To dwell with a people whom God had assigned
The honor of bearing his workto mankind.

Devoted in childhood and faithful in youth,
Your footsteps have trod in the pathway of truth
With hand ever ready the weakest to guide,
As they drifted along on life’s billowy tide.

Your voice has been raised in the conflict of right,
In sunshine of day and darkness of night,
Most freely you’ve striven earth’s children to
	bless,
In times of rejoicing or deepest distress.

Priceless the seed in this life you sow,
Priceless the fruits thy harvest shall know;
Seeds that will spring forth in glory and might,
In a crown for thy head in the mansions of light.

Thy works all await thee on Heaven’s bright.
	shore,
The blessings of Zion have, too, gone before,
The thanks of the hungry thy hand hath fed,
The prayers of the souls thy counsel hath led.

A crown all unfading, eternal, divine,
Where thorns nevermore with roses will twine;
Where nevermore bitter will mingle with sweet
Nor rough be the path, nor weary the feet.

May you live till God’s people are ransomed and
	free,
Live till the wicked from Zion shall flee,
Till the warfare is ended, the victory won,
Then hear the glad tidings “Thy work is well
	done.”
				SARAH A. ROBISON.


SOME REMINISCENCES,

	[Extracts from a letter of Eliza Clayton
Margetts written from Paris, Idaho.]
	AT the age of ten years I, with my par-
ents, emigrated from England and arrived
at Nauvoo in the fall of 1840. Our family
being large, father was counseled by the
Prophet Joseph to go into the country. We
did so and remained away from Nauvoo un-
til the summer of 1841, when we moved
back on account of the persecutions of mob-
ocrats.
	I have a distinct recollection of hearing
Ruth, wife of William Clayton, talk of the
organization of the Female Relief Society,
on her return home from the meeting at
which said organization took place.
	We moved to Carthage in the fall of 1842,
at which place we lived until after the
martyrdom of the Prophet Joseph and his
brother Hyrum. In the forenoon of the
day on which these atrocious murders were
committed, some of the neighbors disguised
and with painted faces, came to our house
and told mother she had better get out of
the way as they were going to kill the Pro-
phet that day. A terrible storm arose and
in the afternoon we heard the firing of guns
and soon after saw some of the murderers run
away howling like fiends. My sister Lucy
who was at this time living with the jailor’s
family and was at the jail when the shoot-
ing commenced, came home and told what
had happened. The next day I went with
my sister Lucy to the jail, we found the
doors and windows open and everything in
confusion, as though the people had left in
great haste. We went up stairs to the room
in which the Prophet and his brother had
been shot. There was some Church books
on the table and portraits of Joseph’s and
Hyrum’s families on the mantlepiece.
There was blood in pools on the floor and
bespattered on the walls, at sight of which
we were overcome with grief and burst in-
to tears. After becoming somewhat col-


lected, we gathered up what we supposed
belonged to the inmates of the room at the
time of the murder and placed it together
on a trunk that was in the room.
	About three weeks after the massacre of
Joseph and Hyrum we moved back to Nau-
voo. I witnessed the trying scenes the
Saints passed through until they were driv-
en from Nauvoo. I remember a circum-
stance that occurred during what is called
the battle of Nauvoo. While at the well
drawing a bucket of water, a cannon ball
from the enemies’ guns struck the chimney
of the house in which we lived, and so
much frightened me that I got my fingers
fast in the well windlass and in extracting
myself skinned my fingers nearly the whole
length.
	I was among the remnant of the sick and
dying Saints on the banks of the Missis-
sippi after the expulsion, when they were
miraculously fed on quails that alighted in
their midst. When the quails alighted they
lit on our laps and everything around.
After we had caught enough to eat they
flew towards the west like a swarm of bees.
Before this some young men went out into
the woods with their guns to find some
game for their families to eat, and while
they were gone the quail came and had
gone when the got back. They had bad
luck while they were out and killed nothing
and had to come back without anything to
eat for their families, and great was their
joy when they found everybody had plenty
to eat.—Deseret Evening News.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.
   .


[Column 1]
R. S. REPORTS.

REORGANIZATION.
	I write to inform you of the reorganiza-
tion of the Relief Society of St. John’s
ward, which occurred March 25, 1898.
Anna D. Kempe who had served faithfully
for four years as president, being a widow
and having to work very hard to support
her family felt she was unable to perform
her duty in that position, and therefore
tendered her resignation which was accepted
and she was honorably released. She is a
very energetic woman and is well respected
by the members of the Relief Society as
president, also by the community at large.
	President E. L. S. Udall, Stake president of
the Relief Society being here at the time, a
special meeting was called and President
Udall together with her counselor, Emily
Patterson and the Bishop of the ward, select-
ed the following officers: Margaret Jarvis,
president; Anna L. Anderson, first counsel-
or; Eleanor Patterson, second counselor;
R. B. Berry, secretary; Sina Stradling,
assistant secretary; Sarah Berry, treasurer.
					R. E. BERRY, Sec.
ST. JOSEPH STAKE. 
	The quarterly conference of the Re-
lief Society of St. Joseph Stake, Arizona,
convened at Thatcher, March 11, 1898,
Counselor Mary L. Ransom presiding in the
absence of the president, Elizabeth Layton,
who was sustained in that position at the
late reorganization of the Stake, President
Wilmirth East who has filled the office so
long and so well, being honorably released
on account of her advanced age and feeble-
ness.
	After opening exercises and reading of
minutes Counselor Ransom made a few re-
marks. Verbal reports of the several

[Column 2]
branches were given by their representative
presidents or counselors and listened to
with much interest by all present. Visits of
the Stake officers were reported by Counsel-
or Ransom, showing an improvement in all
of the branches of the Society in the Stake,
and a desire to continue in the good work.
She spoke in an interesting and impressive
manner on the duties of officers and mem-
bers of the Relief Society, and closed by
praying the blessings of God might be up-
on all.
	President Andrew Kimball gave excellent
counsel to the sisters, in which he said wo-
men have a great influence and should use
it for good.
	After singing, and prayer by Bishop John
Taylor, conference adjourned until 2 p. m.
	At 12:10 the officers met at the residence
of President Elizabeth Layton, receiving
instructions from Counselor Delia Curtis,
President Elizabeth Layton, President Wil-
liam Johnson and President Andrew Kimball.
	At 2 p. m. conference re-convened.
After singing, and prayer by Bishop George
Skinner, statistical and financial reports
were read. The names of the central board
and Stake officers were presented by Coun-
selor Delia Curtis and unanimously sus-
tained, after which she read a selection
from the Millennial Star.
	Stake president of the Primary, Rebecca
Warren, next addressed the conference, with
Annie Yorgan of Mexico, Secretary Salina
Phillips, Patriarch Samuel Claridge, Presi-
dent W. D. Johnson, Bishop Isaac Zundel
and Treasurer E. Allred following with a
recital of such experiences and testimonies
as would tend to strengthen the faith of all
the Latter-day Saints, praying the Lord to
bless us that we might have power in the
land for good. Benediction was pronounced
by Bishop C. B. Boren.
			SARAH WEBB, Cor. Sec.
SAN JUAN.
	Minutes of the Stake conference of the
Relief Society of San Juan. Conference
convened at Bluff, Feb. 20, 1898, President
Martha Hammond presiding. Opened with
the usual exercises.
	President Martha Hammond said she
hoped the Lord would pour out a goodly
portion of His Spirit upon the sisters that
we might be blessed and built up during
our conference and better qualified for our
labors and duties in the future as wives,
mothers and Relief Society workers. Re-
ported her labors among the different
branches of the society.
	The several branches in the Stake were
reported as follows: President Kisten Neil-
son said the Bluff ward Relief Society was
in good condition, the sisters being on hand
to donate when necessary and trying to im-
prove and advance in all that is good.
	President Norah Hammond said the mem-
bers of the Mancos society attended when
possible, but on account of the settlement
being scattered it was not possible for
mothers with families of small children to
attend regularly in the winter season, and
for the same reason meetings were not al-
ways held regularly. Said she felt blest
and well paid for the sacrifices she had
made to attend the quarterly conference.
	President Mary M. Jones reported the
Monticello society as being on the improve
during the last six months.
	A letter was read from the president of
the Fruitland branch stating that the soci-
ety was in good condition, a good spirit pre-
vailing among the sisters.

[Column 3]
Stake Counselor Mary N. Jones had been
greatly blest in attending our conference.
Said if the instructions that had been given
in regard to the training of children were
remembered and practiced we would have
better communities and better boys and
girls. Counselor Eliza Redd followed in
the same strain as the previous speakers
in regard to the training of children, and
gave some very good instructions on that
subject.
	Sister Delia Lyman said she was glad that
mothers were waking up to a sense of their
duties and seeing the necessity of keeping
their little boys close around them as they
do their little girls; for they learn many evil
things by roaming the streets.
	Elder Adam Peterson, one of the Young
Mens’ M. I. A. missionaries, was the next
speaker. He said he felt that we could not
do without the Relief Society; spoke elo-
quently of the duties of husbands to wives
and of sons to mothers and sisters. He felt
that woman was worthy of much more than
many gave her credit for; to her we were
indebted for the better and finer parts of our
being. Spoke at some length on obedience
to parents and the priesthood.
	The minutes of the previous conference
were read and approved, after which the
General and Stake officers were unanimous-
ly sustained. President F. A. Hammond en-
dorsed the remarks that had been made.
President Martha Hammond made a few
closing remarks, hoped the sisters would
feel blest in coming to conference, even if
they did have to travel over bad roads.
Conference adjourned for three months,
to be held at Fruitland. The choir sang
“Guide us O Thou Great Jehovah,” and 
Bishop Jens Neilson pronounced the bene-
diction. 
				EVELYN ADAMS, Sec.
SANPETE STAKE.
	Minutes of the Relief Society conference
of Sanpete Stake held in Ephraim, March
11, 1898, President M. A. Hyde presiding.
Singing “Come, come ye Saints.” Open-
ing prayer by President Peterson. Singing
“We thank Thee O God for a prophet.” The
following branches were represented: Eph-
raim North ward, Fairview, Mt. Pleasant,
Manti North and South wards, Moroni,
Chester, Ephraim South ward, Mayfield,
Sterling and Freedom. Fountain Green,
Spring City, Milburn, Indianola, Gunnison
and Centerfield were not reported.
	Opening remarks were made by Presi-
dent M. A. Hyde Sister Emmeline B.
Wells spoke at some length upon the neces-
sity of entertaining a spirit of humility and
obedience. Related her impressions when
she first saw the Prophet Joseph.
	President J. D. T. McAllister was the
next speaker. He said he felt that we were
greatly blest in having the privilege of
meeting together, and that we were ac-
quainted with each other before we came
here to prepare for the life to come. Said
the Relief Society was one of the great
helps of the Church and thanked God for it.
Exhorted the sisters to reach out a helping
hand to those who were in affliction, and to
strive to exert a saving influence over those
who were falling by the wayside. Admon-
ished the young people to be pure and
virtuous. Dwelt on the necessity of the
young people of Zion entering the marriage
relation in a proper manner, and invoked
the Divine blessing upon the sisters.
	Singing “Guide Us O, Thou Great Jeho-


[Column 1]
vah.” Benediction by Bishop Anderson.
	Afternoon session. Singing “Now let
us rejoice.” Opening prayer by Brother J.
F. Dorius. President Hyde was pleased
with the reports of the various wards. En-
couraged the storing of grain, and all good
works pertaining to the Relief Society.
	Sister Annie Hyde was pleased to have
another opportunity of meeting with the
sisters of Sanpete, and urged the necessity
of a spirit of love and charity. Said that
the Spirit of the Lord would not dwell
where there was strife and contention. Ex-
horted the mothers to train their children in
the way they should go to become faithful
Latter-day Saints. Also referred to the
Prophet Joseph.
	President M. A. Hyde spoke words of
comfort and blessing to President Peterson
and the sisters in the gift of tongues, which
was interpreted by Sister Wells.
	President Peterson said “We are living in
an interesting age of the world, but the
signs of the times indicate that the end is
near.” Also made some remarks on the
life of the Prophet Elijah.
	Elder Andrew Jensen was the next
speaker. He dwelt upon the duties and re-
sponsibilities of mothers in training the
children when the fathers were absent in
the mission field. Spoke of the danger of
allowing scoffers and unbelievers to associate
with our children, and related a circum-
stance of this kind which had come under
his observation. Eulogized the good
work of the Relief Society.
	“MRS. ANNIE M. BROWN, of Brattleboro,
has been appointed by Attorney General
Griggs as clerk of the United States Attor-
ney’s Department for the district of Ver-
mont. Most of the depositions and other
legal testimony taken stenographically in
Brattleboro and vicinity for some time past
has been taken by Mrs. Brown, and she is
regarded as fully competent.”
	Sister Wells said we should improve in
our duties, but should work on the founda-
tion which has been laid. Paid tribute to
the sterling virtues and good works of Sis-
ter Eliza R. Snow and others of the aged
and faithful sisters who have labored so
long. Considered the Relief Society the
greatest organization of women in the
world. Was pleased to state that we had
branches of the Relief Society in Chicago,
California, Holland and Scandinavia. She
spoke of the necessity of encouraging the
silk industry.
	Singing “Our God we raise to Thee.”
Benediction by J. F. Dorius.

	SATURDAY MORNING, MARCH 12.

	Opening hymn “Softly beams the sacred
dawning.” Prayer by Prof. Newton Noyes.
	President M. A. Hyde expressed her
pleasure at seeing so many young ladies
present, and urged them to attend the meet-
ings of the Relief Society. Warned them
against the evils that are abroad in the land
and told them to guard their virtue as a
precious gem.
	Stake Counselor Abbie Shoemaker was
also pleased to see the girls. Exhorted the
sisters to greater faith and humility, and
urged them to teach their children the
principles of the Gospel, especially faith.
Said mothers should warn their children
against the temptations that beset the
young.
	Sister Annie T. Hyde was the next
speaker. She referred to the pleasure she
had experienced the day before in visiting
the Primary. Said it was necessary that

[Column 2]
the young ladies attend the meetings of the
Relief Society, and dwelt at some length on
the subject of love and charity as a means
of binding Satan, as he would no longer
have any influence over the Latter-day
Saints. Said there was no dividing line
between the Relief Society and the Young
Ladies, that they were mother and daugh-
ter. Related some incidents which had oc-
cured on President Woodruff’s birthday,
notably a vision which he had seen similar
to that of John the Revelator concerning
the judgments of the last days.
	Stake Counselor A. L. Cox thanked the
young ladies for responding to the invita-
tion to be present; also thanked the various
Relief Society presidents for their prompt-
ness in responding to every call from the
Stake presidency. Compared the faith and
belief of the Latter-day Saints with that of
the different Christian churches, and by
way of illustration referred to a funeral
which she had attended where a Presbyter-
ian minister had officiated. Exhorted the
sisters to renewed diligence in subscribing
for the “Exponent,” that it may have the
necessary support to continue its publication.
	Sister Wells also expressed her pleasure
at meeting with the young ladies. Said
they could not fail to be benefited, and said
that a spirit had manifested itself in
some localities among the young people that
the Relief Society was an old fogy institu-
tion, but that it was an altogether wrong
impression and that the experience of the
older sisters was of great value to the
younger generation, and that the Relief
Society was at the head of all the other
associations. Explained some of the diffi-
culties surrounding the publication of the
“Exponent” unless it was liberally pat-
ronized. Gave some of her experiences in
early Relief Society work, and dwelt upon
the necessity of the Relief Society owning
property and storing grain. Said that it
was very necessary that the sisters pray for
Sister Zina Young as she had great re-
sponsibilities.
	President Hyde blessed the congregation
with a mother’s blessing, and urged the
young sisters to be diligent in the things of
the Gospel.
	President Peterson said he had been more
interested in the teachings of the sisters than
ever, hoped they would come again soon.
Said the sisters had built up the kingdom
as much as the brethren, and the wives of
the missionaries were performing as great a
work as the missionaries themselves. Felt
that he could talk for hours if time would
permit.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Benediction by President Peterson.
SOPHIA JENSEN, Act. Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

MARTHA PETERSON HOLM:
	Sister Martha Peterson Holm, daughter of
Peter and Mari Ane Christesen, was born March
21, 1849, in Hytter, Denmark. She emigrated to
Utah in 1873, and was married to Andrew Holm
at Salt Lake City Nov 11, 1873 and lived at Lake
Shore where she resided at the time of her death,
March 9, 1898. She was a teacher in the Relief
Society of Lake Shore, a faithful Latter-day Saint
and was loved and respected by all who knew her.
She leaves a kind husband and five children, four
daughters and one son to mourn her loss.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	WHEREAS, Our Heavenly Father has seen fit to
take from our midst one of our sisters, be it
	RESOLVED, That we the officers and members
of the Relief Society of Lake Shore do extend our


heartfelt sympathy to the bereaved family in this
their hour of sorrow, and t[-]at we ever cherish
her memory.
	RESOLVED. That a copy of these resolutions be
presented [-]o the bereaved family, one placed on
the records of th[-] Relief Society and one sent to
the “Woman’s Exponent” for publication.
PHILLIS BECKSTROM,
MARY A. JONES,
CHRISTENA HOLM,
Committee.

ESTHER ANN ROLFSON.

	It is with the deepest regret that we are called
upon to record the death of our beloved sister,
Esther Ann Rolfson, who died at her home in
American Fork. Feb. 15, 1898. Sister Rolfson was
born at Savannah, Andrews Co., Missouri, May
1, 1851, her parents were Joseph and Hannah
Pulley. She became the wife of Frank Rolfson by
whom she had four children, three of whom sur-
vive her, two sons and one daughter. Sister
Rolfson was a member of the Relief Society for
many years, and at our last election was chosen
one of the board of directors, and proved herself
worthy of the position. She was always on hand
and willing to do her share of whatever work was
assigned her. She was true and faithful to her
convictions and loyal to the cause she had es-
poused, instructing her children in the truth of
the everlasting Gospel; her family are indeed a
credit to her, for she was a devoted wife and
mother, and although suffering intensely from
that dread disease cancer, she never murmured
but always had a word of counsel and encourag-
ment for her children, and in her intense suffer-
ing had unbounded faith in the administration for
the sick, from which she always received a benefit.
	Her loss will be keen y felt by her beloved
daughter who was untiring in her devotion to her
mother during her long illness. We truly sympa-
thize with the family of Sister Rolfson in their
great loss and trust they will have that comfort
and assistance that can only be found by bowing
in submission to the will of our Heavenly Father.
ELLETHERIA D. ROBINSON,
ELLEN BARRATT,
EMMA FEATHERSTONE,
Committee.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


CLARA BARTON ARRIVES.

	TAMPA Fla., April 12.—The steamship
Olivette, which arrived here from Havana
brought 250 refugees from Cuba, also Miss
Barton, president of the Red Cross Society
and her staff. Miss Barton said:
	“It is the duty of the Red Cross Society
to follow in the wake of the army rather
than to lead it, and wherever duty calls
there you will find us. In the present in-
stance we simply deemed discretion the
better part of valor. The original treaty of
Geneva provides only for relief in war. The
extensions of the provisions of the treaty
to include other disasters was a special con-
cession made to the American Red Cross
upon its accession to the treaty and known
as the American amendment.
	"The singular coincidence now presents
itself of both provisions of the treaty being
called into action in the same field. The
Red Cross has distributed the food sent by
the people of America to suffering recon-
centrados as long as such action was admis-
sible. Now that war is imminent and our
guns prepared to sweep the island it be-
comes the duty of the Red Cross to retire
from the threatened field and place itself in
position behind the guns and wait.”
	Since arriving here Miss Barton has re-
ceived from prominent men in New York
requests that the society lend its aid to alle-
viate the suffering on the part of the desti-
tute Cuban element of this city. Miss Bar-
ton will have a consultation with the May-
or of the city, which will probably result in
her securing speedier relief for the sufferers.


TWO of Tennyson’s sisters are living, the
youngest in her 81st year.

	THE March number of Jeness Miller's
Monthly pays a warm tribute to the work
of "Jennie June" Croly among the Womens’
Clubs. Mrs. Croly is at work upon a "His-
tory of the Woman’s Club Movement in
America" which is to be published under
the auspices of the General Federation.

	A DATE in mid-September, 1898, will
probably be the day upon which Queen
Wilhelmina of Holland will formally take
the oath of accession to the Dutch throne.
No one could have shown more tact in
managing affairs of State during her daugh-
ter’s minority than the Queen Regent, who
like her sister, the Duchess of Albany, is a
person of singularly unpretentious manners
and simple tastes. This is shown in her
daily habits and surroundings. Her Majes-
ty, for instance, although for more formal
communications she uses thick white writ-
ing paper with the crown and royal coat
of arms emblazoned in gold and scarlet and
blue, uses for everyday familiar letters,
paper merely stamped with her name
"Emma," and a crown. In dress also she
is inclined to simplicity, and she follows, in
preference to leading the fashion.

A DREAM.

	A LO[-]G, long time ago when fairies and
brownies were flourishing there was in this
world a large meeting, and among them
was the fairy Opaline who decided to visit
the earth accompanied by her faithful
brownie, Amethyst. The aim of her trip
was to distribute among women a special
gift for each nationality. Amethyst bore
the casket containing the precious treasure.
On reaching the earth Amethyst issued the
call for a young woman of each nation to
appear before the fairy, and soon the throne
of Opaline was surrounded by a crowd of
women.
	The good fairy explained kindly that her
wish was to bestow upon each a token of
which she might be proud. "I cannot
give to all the same gift," said she, "but
you will appreciate them better, because if
all were uniform they would become mono-
tonous." A few words more and the speech
was ended and the distribution began.
	The first young woman to bow before
the throne of the good fairy was a Spanish
girl, and she received the gift of hair so
black and so long that without difficulty
she could wrap herself therein as in a mantle.
	The Italian came bowing and was given
eyes so sparkling and so ardent as to com-
pare with the eruption of Vesuvius by night.
	A Turkish beauty followed and upon her
was bestowed the roundness of the moon,
and a smoothness like the bird's feathers.
	Next came a Swedish lady and received a
complexion as fair as an Aurora Borealis.
	Then the German girl approached and the
kind fairy gave her teeth as perfect as her
own and gifted her with a sensible heart,
deeply disposed to love.
	To the Russian she gave a queenly bearing.
Afterward came the Persian. Opaline
put gaiety on her lips, wit and common
sense in her head and kindness in her heart.
	The casket being empty the fairy made
ready to fly again to the upper regions,
when a beautiful girl approached blushing
and taking her by her gossamer tunic said,
"Did you forget me, dear fairy, that you
gave me nothing?" The sweet, musical
voice charmed the fairy, who turned and
said kindly, "And who are you?" "I am
the American." "Oh, said Opaline, "you
were too near—I did not see you, and now
I have nothing left to give." But without
a moment's hesitation she ordered Amethyst
to recall those who had been gifted and
when they were assembled she said, "I am
guilty of a great forgetfulness but you can
help me to rectify my mistake. I have en-
tirely forgotten your American sister, and I
will be pleased if each of you will give to
her a portion of your gift. It will be a
small loss for you and will greatly compen-
sate her for the disappointment I have
caused." Well as all were very grateful to
the good fairy they could not refuse, and
with the exquisite grace which ordinarily
all happy people possess, they came one
after another to the American girl and gave,
one some of her splendid hair, another
some roses from her complexion, another
some of her gaiety and brightness; all were
generous, and in conclusion the American,
at first unseen, was by this act of partaking
better gifted than any of her comrades.
	And this is the reason why among all the
civilized nations the American woman
wears on her head the crown of beauty and
in her own country her name is written in
letters of gold in the annals of history.
EUGENIE CLEOPHAS.

THE FLAG GOES BY.
		BY H. H. BENNETT.

	HATS off!
Along the street there comes
A blare of bugles, a ruffle of drums,
A flash of color beneath the sky;
	Hats off!
The flag is passing by!

Blue and crimson and white it shines,
Over the steel-tipped ordered lines.
	Hats off!
The colors before us fly:
But more than the flag is passing by.

 Sea-fights and land-fights grim and great,
Fought to make and to save the State.
Weary marches and sinking ships;
Cheers of victory on dying lips;

Days of plenty and years of peace;
March of a strong land’s swift increase;
Equal justice, right and law,
Stately honor and reverend awe;

Sign of a nation great and strong,
Go ward her people from foreign wrong;
Pride and glory and honor, all
Live in the colors to stand or fall.

	Hats off!
Along the street there comes
A blare of bug'es, a ruffle of drums;
And loyal hearts are beating high:
	Hats off!
The flag is passing by!
				-Youth's Companion.


(Column 1)
	MARTIN LUTHER.

THIS wonderful Reformer,
	I’ve chosen for my theme,
Altho’ his name has echoed
	And re-echoed down the stream
Of time, while bards and orators 
	Have sung and spoke his praise,
With eloquence enchanting,
	And with sweet, harmonious lays.

Most surely was the part he played
	In the history of our race,
All planned by an Almighty power,
	E’en the very time and place,
That he should do his brave, bold deeds, 
	And to help the boy, was given
A lovely, noble mother,
	To teach of God and heaven.

(Column 2)
Amid, a world of chaos
	Where all was dark and dim;
As a brilliant, fiery meteor
	He shot forth, and to him;
All generations owe
	Their love and homage true,
For to him the precious liberty
	We have is largely due.
With al[-] his powers he battled
	’Gainst the false and for the true.

His deep religious nature
	And the evil he had known,
Made him separa e from his fellows,
	And seek the covenant lone. 
While here his soul found respite
	From the burden of his sin,
As he learned the Savior’s mission
	That salvation he might win.

The burning words he uttered, 
	Came not from lips alone,
But from the soul’s recesses,
	And from reasons lofty throne.
So they touched the hearts of others
	With convincing mighty power,
And the march of truth swept onward
 	Gaining converts hour by hour.

E’en the angels seemed to aid him
	As they soared on wings of love,
Bearing with them Luther’s message,
	Inspired from above.
And when the Church assailed him,
	He bravely stood his ground,
True to God and conscience,
	Though demons hovered  [-]ound.

As the keen and vivid lightning
	He pierced proud error’s shield,
Aggressive, bold defiant
	He compelled the foe to yield,
Or like the awful thunder
	Pealing loud from height to height,
He swayed the fate of nations,
	And stood fearless in his might.

Yet this brave and gifted leader
	Was tender in his love,
 In his care for wife and children
	He was gentle as a dove,
In his character was mingled
	The stern, the fierce, the kind,
Defiant when beset by wrong,
	Yet graceful and refined.

He knew not fear but fear of God,
	Which garnished all his life, 
Where’er that led him there he walked,
	However fierce the strife,
Though mighty powers rebuked him,
	Defiant, grand and bold,
He scorned them, taking for his shield,
	The Scriptures had of old.

And from that flame he kindled
	Has grown a brilliant fire,
Blazing aloft unto the heavens,
	While all the world admire.
The good will ever honor him
	And on the scroll of fame,
In letters bright will ever glisten
	Martin Luther’s name.
			MARY A. FREEZE.
